processingNotes: OpenXML documents are ZIP-based archives containing XML parts; the filter processes internal XML parts transparently.
The filter supports Word (DOCX/DOCM/DOTX/DOTM), Excel (XLSX/XLSM/XLTX/XLTM), PowerPoint (PPTX/PPTM/PPSX/PPSM/POTX/POTM), and Visio (VSDX/VSDM) formats.
Text excluded via highlight colors is treated as hidden — the 'Translate Hidden' options will still extract it.
The 'Clean Tags Aggressively' option is specifically designed for documents converted from PDF where extra formatting noise is common.
Starting in 1.48.0, the Excluded/Included Highlight Colors option from Word configuration also applies to PowerPoint content.

examples.title: Excel Worksheet Configuration: Source/Target Columns with Excluded Rows
examples.description: Configures translation of column A into column B on Sheet1, excluding the first two header rows and columns C and D entirely.
examples.output: After extraction and merge, column A content (A3, A4, A5) is copied to column B with translations (A3-tr, A4-tr, A5-tr). Rows 1-2 and columns C-D remain untouched.

examples.title: Excel Worksheet Configuration: Metadata Rows and Columns
examples.description: Configures extraction of columns A and B for translation, with column D as metadata context and rows 1-2 as metadata headers. Row 5 is excluded entirely.
examples.output: Rows 1-2 are treated as metadata headers. Column D values appear as context-group metadata in the XLIFF for each translatable row. Row 5 is excluded from extraction.

examples.title: Excel Custom RGB Color Exclusion
examples.description: Excludes a custom RGB color (#69b3e7, Pantone 292) from translation by modifying the configuration file directly, since this color is not in the standard Excel 2010 palette UI.
examples.output: Cells with foreground or background color #69b3e7 are excluded from translation extraction.

okf_pdf.overview: The PDF Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for PDF files. It extracts text from PDF documents but does not handle complex formatting like tables or multi-level lists. The typical use case is to scrape text from PDFs for quick word counts and leverage analysis. Importantly, this filter does not merge back into PDF format — it produces plain text file output upon merging.

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling settings. When enabled, regex patterns are used to identify inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within the extracted PDF text. Sub-properties control whether adjacent codes are merged, whether boundary codes are moved to the skeleton, and define the regex rules for detection.
notes: The wiki states 'This filter has no parameters', suggesting inline code support was added after the wiki was last updated.

whitespace.description: Whitespace handling settings for extracted PDF text. Controls whether original whitespace is preserved, whether multiple whitespace characters are collapsed into single spaces, and whether leading/trailing whitespace is trimmed from text segments.
notes: PDF files often contain irregular whitespace due to the way text is positioned on the page. These settings help normalize the extracted text.

parser.preserveWhitespace.description: When enabled, preserves the original whitespace in extracted text from the PDF. When disabled (the default), whitespace may be normalized during extraction.

parser.indentThreshold.description: Defines the amount of indent (in PDF coordinate units) needed to define a new paragraph. The default value is 2.0. When text is indented by more than this threshold relative to the previous line, it is treated as the start of a new paragraph and a new TextUnit is created.
notes: Value is a string representation of a floating-point number.

parser.spacingTolerance.description: Defines the amount of spacing (in PDF coordinate units) needed to define a whitespace character between glyphs. The default value is 0.5. This controls how the filter determines word boundaries when extracting text from PDF content streams where characters are individually positioned.
notes: Value is a string representation of a floating-point number.

output.lineSeparator.description: The character or character sequence to use as a line separator in the extracted output. Defaults to a single newline character (\n).

output.paragraphSeparator.description: The character or character sequence to use as a paragraph separator in the extracted output. Defaults to a single newline character (\n). Paragraphs are determined by the indent threshold setting.

sortByPosition.description: When enabled, sorts the extracted text by its position within the PDF document. This can help produce more readable output when the PDF's internal content stream order does not match the visual reading order. When disabled (the default), text is extracted in the order it appears in the PDF content stream.

limitations: This filter merges back in plain text format, not PDF. The output upon merging is a plain text file, not a reconstructed PDF document.
processingNotes: PDF files are binary files and do not have a specific encoding. Okapi extracts all text from the PDF as a Java string and forces the encoding to be UTF-16. Any encoding selected in tools like Rainbow will be ignored.
TextUnits are created following the default rules of the Plain Text filter — any text followed by a newline will create a new TextUnit or paragraph.

okf_pensieve.overview: The Pensieve TM Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for Pensieve TMs. It allows you to use a Pensieve TM as an input or output document, just like other translatable documents such as TMX, PO, etc.
limitations: The method setOutput(OutputStream output) of the PensieveFilterWriter class is not implemented because Pensieve TM content is binary. You cannot output the content of a Pensieve TM to other types of stream without going through a format conversion.
processingNotes: The filter always uses its internal encoding, so any user-specific setting for encoding is ignored.

okf_phpcontent.overview: The PHP Content Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for PHP content. The implementation is based on the PHP syntax found in the PHP language Reference documentation. This filter is not meant to process HTML files with PHP content, but rather the content of the PHP tags themselves — many files with a .php extension are actually HTML files with PHP tags. It extracts translatable strings from PHP constructs including simple strings, heredoc/nowdoc syntax, and array declarations.

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling. When enabled, the specified regular expressions are applied to the text of extracted items, and any match is converted to an inline code. The default expressions detect: partial HTML tags at start/end of strings, HTML-like tags, escape sequences (\a, \b, \f, \n, \r, \t, \v), email addresses, and placeholder patterns in square or curly brackets.

useDirectives.description: Enable the filter to recognize localization directives when they are present. Localization directives are special comments that override the default extraction behavior of the filter. The syntax and behavior of the directives are the same across all Okapi filters. If this option is not set, any localization directive in the input file will be ignored and all extractable strings will be extracted.
notes: Note: directives override key conditions.

extractOutsideDirectives.description: Extract any translatable item that is not within the scope of a localization directive. Selecting to extract or not outside localization directives allows you to mark up fewer parts of the source document. For example, if most strings should be extracted, enable this option and use directives only to exclude specific strings. Conversely, if most strings should not be extracted, disable this option and use directives only to mark the strings that should be extracted.

dependsOn.condition: must be true (enabled only when 'Use localization directives' is set)

limitations: Support for the define statement is not implemented yet.
In array declarations, both string key and string value are extracted.
processingNotes: Encoding detection priority: (1) Unicode BOM if present, (2) charset declaration in first 1000 characters of file, (3) default encoding from filter options. If BOM encoding and header charset conflict, a warning is generated and BOM encoding takes precedence.
If the charset declaration value is literally 'charset' (case insensitive), the file is treated as a template with no encoding declared, and auto-detected or default encoding is used.
Output encoding: if the file has a header charset declaration, it is automatically updated to reflect the output encoding.
UTF-8 BOM handling: BOM is preserved in output only if input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM. If converting from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8, no BOM is added.
Line-break type in output matches the original input line-break type.

examples.title: PHP content with extractable strings
examples.description: Shows a PHP file with various string constructs. Extractable text includes heredoc strings, simple quoted strings in assignments and arrays, and heredoc strings with embedded variables. The filter extracts the string content while preserving PHP variable references and expressions.
examples.input:
<?php
$str = <<<EOD
Example of string
spanning multiple lines
using heredoc syntax.
EOD;

class foo
{
    var $foo;
    var $bar;

    function foo()
    {
        $this->foo = 'Foo';
        $this->bar = array('Bar1', 'Bar2', 'Bar3');
    }
}

$foo = new foo();
$name = 'MyName';

echo <<<EOT
My name is "$name". I am printing some $foo->foo.
Now, I am printing some {$foo->bar[1]}.
This should print a capital 'A': \x41
EOT;
?>

okf_plaintext.overview: The Plain Text filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for plain text documents. It processes text files encoded in ANSI, Unicode, UTF-8, and UTF-16, with byte-order mark detection. The filter provides three extraction modes: by paragraphs (groups of lines separated by empty lines), by individual lines, and by custom regex rules. It also supports spliced lines (continuation lines in source code) and inline code detection via regular expressions.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation. These rules control how inline codes are simplified in the extracted text, reducing complex code patterns to simpler placeholder representations for easier translation.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The filter decides input encoding by first checking for a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM). If a BOM is found, the corresponding encoding (UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.) is used. Otherwise, the default encoding specified in filter options is used.
For UTF-8 output: if the input was also UTF-8, a BOM is written only if one was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is written to the output.
The type of line-breaks in the output is preserved to match the original input.

examples.title: Extract by paragraphs
examples.description: When 'Extract by paragraphs' mode is used, groups of lines separated by one or more empty lines are extracted as single text units. Line breaks within a paragraph are treated according to the Multi-line text units settings.

examples.title: Extract by lines
examples.description: Every line of text is extracted as a separate text unit. This mode also supports spliced lines where a splicer character (backslash, underscore, or custom) at the end of a line indicates continuation on the next line.

examples.title: Extract with regex rule (line-based)
examples.description: Uses a Java regular expression to extract translatable text. The source group parameter defines which capture group contains the text to extract.
examples.input:
Rule: (^(?=.+))(.*?)$
Source group: 2
Regex options: MULTILINE

Input:
This is the first sentence. And this is the second one.
Second paragraph. Each one ends at the line-break.
examples.output: Each line is extracted as a separate text unit based on the regex match.

examples.title: Extract with regex rule (paragraph-based)
examples.description: Uses a regex pattern that matches text between double newlines to extract paragraphs.
examples.input:
Rule: (\n*)(.*?)(\n\n|\Z)
Source group: 2
Regex options: DOTALL | MULTILINE

Input:
First paragraph line one.
First paragraph line two.

Second paragraph.
examples.output: Two text units are extracted, one per paragraph block.

examples.title: Spliced lines with backslash
examples.description: In 'Extract by lines' mode, lines ending with a backslash are merged with the following line. The splicer character can optionally be replaced with an inline code placeholder.
examples.input:
This is a long \
line of text
examples.output: Extracted as a single text unit: 'This is a long <x1/>line of text' (with inline code for the splicer)

okf_po.overview: The PO Filter implements the IFilter interface for Gettext PO (Portable Object) resource files and POT (PO templates). It is based on the PO specifications from the GNU gettext manual and supports bilingual and monolingual processing modes, plural forms, domains, and various PO comment types. The filter handles encoding detection via BOM and charset declarations, preserves original line-break types, and provides access to PO metadata such as references, extracted comments, translator comments, and context lines.

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling. When enabled, the specified regular expressions are applied to extracted text items, and any match is converted to an inline code. The default expression matches printf-style format specifiers (e.g., %s, %d), common escape sequences (\n, \r, \t, etc.), and numbered placeholders (e.g., {1}).
notes: The default regex pattern covers three categories: printf format specifiers like %d/%s/%f, escape sequences like \n/\r/\t, and curly-brace placeholders like {1}.

mode.description: Processing mode settings that control how the PO file is interpreted — either as a bilingual file (source in msgid, translation in msgstr) or as a monolingual file (real ID in msgid, text in msgstr).

bilingualMode.description: When enabled, the input document is processed as a bilingual file where the msgid entry is the source text and the msgstr entry is the translation. This is the normal standard way PO files are processed. When disabled, the file is treated as monolingual where the msgid entry is a real identifier (rather than the source text) and the corresponding text is in the msgstr entry. In monolingual mode, the msgid value is used as the identifier and set as the name of the extracted resource.

protectApproved.description: When enabled, all entries that are not empty and not fuzzy are set with a non-translatable flag, protecting approved translations from changes. The fuzzy flag in PO files is indicated by '#, fuzzy' and means the msgstr text is only a proposed translation. In bilingual mode, the filter exposes the fuzzy flag through the 'approved' target property: if the flag is set, approved='no'; if deleted or changed to something other than 'no', the fuzzy flag is removed from the output document.

wrapContent.description: Controls whether long content lines in the output are wrapped. When enabled, long msgid and msgstr lines will be broken across multiple quoted strings, following PO file conventions.

identification.description: ID and naming settings controlling how text unit identifiers are generated and how context information is handled.

makeID.description: When enabled, generates identifiers from the source text of the msgid entry. The values are constructed from the hash code of the source text, possibly with a domain prefix, and possibly with the text of the msgctxt entry. Note that the value may not be unique if the source text is not unique within the same domain or has no distinct context values. The id value is set as the name of the extracted resource.
notes: Generated IDs may not be unique if the source text is not unique within the same domain or has no distinct context values.
The msgctxt entry is used as a differentiator, so two entries with the same source text but different context content will have different identifiers.

dependsOn.condition: must be true (only available in bilingual mode)

includeMsgContextInNote.description: When enabled, the content of the msgctxt (context) entry is included in the notes associated with the text unit. Context entries are denoted by the 'msgctxt' marker in PO files and provide disambiguation context for translators.

output.description: Output formatting settings controlling the format and behavior of generated output files.

outputGeneric.description: When enabled, output is generated in a generic format rather than the standard PO format.

allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: When enabled, allows the target (msgstr) to be empty in the output when no translation is available. When disabled, untranslated entries will retain their original content in the output.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Encoding detection follows a priority chain: (1) Unicode BOM if present, (2) charset declaration in header entry within first 1000 characters, (3) default encoding from filter options. If both BOM and header charset exist and conflict, a warning is generated and the BOM encoding takes precedence.
If the header charset value is literally 'charset' (case insensitive), the file is treated as a template with no declared encoding, and the auto-detected or default encoding is used.

Output encoding: if the file has a header charset declaration, it is automatically updated to reflect the output encoding. For UTF-8 output, a BOM is only included if the input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM.
No language information is updated in the PO header entry. Any language-related information must be updated manually.
Line-break type in the output matches the original input.

Plural forms are supported with the assumption they appear in sequential order (msgstr[0], msgstr[1], etc.). All msgstr strings of a plural entry are processed as a single group with type 'x-gettext-plurals'.
Domains are supported as groups with type 'x-gettext-domain' and the domain name as the group resource name.
Any entry found without quotes in its first line is rewritten with an initial empty string (e.g., bare 'msgid' followed by quoted continuation lines gets an empty string inserted).
If a msgctxt contains an Okapi-format context like 'okpCtx:tu=123', the text unit id is set to the id provided (e.g., 123).
The fuzzy flag (#, fuzzy) maps to the 'approved' target property. When set, approved='no'; removing or changing the property removes the flag from output.
The following flags are currently ignored: No-Wrap Flag, X-Format Flag, No-X-Format Flag.

examples.title: Basic PO file structure
examples.description: A simple PO file with header entry (empty msgid) containing encoding and plural information, followed by translatable entries including a plural form entry.
examples.input:
# PO file for myApp

msgid ""
msgstr ""
"Project-Id-Version: myApp 1.0.0\n"
"Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8\n"
"Plural-Forms: nplurals=2; plural=(n != 1);\n"

msgid "diverging after version %d of %s"
msgstr ""

msgid "You have selected %d file for deletion"
msgid_plural "You have selected %d files for deletion"
msgstr[0] ""
msgstr[1] ""

examples.title: Plural forms extraction to XLIFF
examples.description: Shows how plural form entries (msgid/msgid_plural/msgstr[N]) are extracted as a group with type 'x-gettext-plurals'. Each plural form gets a separate trans-unit with an indexed resource name (e.g., P3ADE34F0-0, P3ADE34F0-1).
examples.output:
<group restype="x-gettext-plurals">
  <trans-unit id="1" resname="P3ADE34F0-0">
    <source>untranslated-plural</source>
    <target>translated-singular</target>
  </trans-unit>
  <trans-unit id="2" resname="P3ADE34F0-1">
    <source>untranslated-plural</source>
    <target>translated-plural-form1</target>
  </trans-unit>
  <trans-unit id="3" resname="P3ADE34F0-2">
    <source>untranslated-singular</source>
    <target>translated-plural-form2</target>
  </trans-unit>
</group>

examples.title: Domain support
examples.description: Shows how domain directives are represented as groups with type 'x-gettext-domain' and the domain name as the resource name.
examples.input:
domain TheDomain1
msgid "Text 1 in domain 'TheDomain1'"
msgstr "Texte 1 dans le domain 'TheDomain1'"
examples.output:
<group resname="TheDomain1" restype="x-gettext-domain">
  <trans-unit id="1" resname="N9D1999AB">
    <source>Text 1 in domain 'TheDomain1'</source>
    <target>Texte 1 dans le domain 'TheDomain1'</target>
  </trans-unit>
</group>

examples.title: Okapi context ID override
examples.description: When msgctxt contains an Okapi-format context string with a tu= parameter, the text unit ID is set to the specified value.
examples.output: The extracted text unit will have id=123.

examples.title: Lines without quotes rewriting
examples.description: Entries with bare msgid/msgstr lines (missing the initial empty quoted string) are automatically rewritten with an initial empty string on the first line.
examples.output:
msgid ""
"source text"
msgstr ""
"target text"

examples.title: Default inline code patterns
examples.description: The default inline code regex expression covers printf format specifiers, escape sequences, and numbered placeholders.

okf_properties.overview: The Properties Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for Java properties files (.properties, .lang). The implementation is based on the specification found in the Java java.util.Properties class documentation, with support for additional features for compatibility with other types of properties files. Each entry in the property file is mapped to a text unit resource, with the key becoming the text unit name and the text becoming the source content.

extraction.useKeyCondition.description: Set this option to extract items based on their keys. When enabled, you specify a regular expression pattern; if the key matches the pattern, the item is extracted or not depending on the extractOnlyMatchingKey setting. The pattern must be a valid regular expression.
notes: Note: directives have precedence over key condition.

extraction.extractOnlyMatchingKey.description: When true, only items with keys matching the keyCondition pattern are extracted. When false, items with keys matching the pattern are excluded from extraction (i.e., do not extract matching items).

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this setting to have any effect

extraction.keyCondition.description: A regular expression pattern to test against the key of each entry. For example, '.*text.*' matches any key containing the word 'text'. The pattern must be a valid Java regular expression.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this pattern to be applied

extraction.localizeOutside.description: Set this option to extract any translatable item that is not within the scope of a localization directive. Selecting to extract or not outside localization directives allows you to mark up fewer parts of the source document.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this option to be enabled

extraction.extraComments.description: Set this option to take into account other comment styles in addition to the strict Java comments (single-line starting with '#' or '!'). When this option is set, the filter also recognizes single-lines starting with ';', as well as single-lines where '//' is the first non-whitespace sequence. Note that '//' after a '=' are considered part of the value of the entry, not a comment.

notes.commentsAreNotes.description: Set this option to include the comments before each entry as a note property on the text unit of the corresponding entry. All comment lines are grouped into a single note.

identification.useLd.description: Use localization directives when they are present. Localization directives are special comments you can use to override the default behavior of the filter regarding the parts to extract. The syntax and behavior of the directives are the same across all Okapi filters.
notes: Note: directives override key conditions.

identification.idLikeResname.description: Set this option to use the keys of the extracted entries as ID for the text-units. Note that the key is already extracted into the name property (resname in XLIFF). Note also that the value of a property key may not be a valid string for some output formats; for example in XLIFF2 the unit ID must be a NMTOKEN value.

output.escapeExtendedChars.description: Set this option to convert all characters above U+007F into Unicode escape sequences (\uHHHH). When this option is not set, only the characters not supported by the output encoding are escaped.

output.useJavaEscapes.description: When enabled, extracted text retains Java Property escaping conventions. Default is false.

output.convertLFandTab.description: Set this option to convert the escaped codes \n and \t to true line-breaks and tabs. All the other escaped characters remain escaped.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Set this option to use the specified regular expressions on the text of the extracted items. Any match will be converted to an inline code. By default the expression matches printf-style format specifiers (%d, %s, etc.), escaped characters (\n, \r, \t, etc.), and Java MessageFormat placeholders ({0}, {1}, etc.).

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regular expression patterns used to identify inline codes in translatable text. The default pattern matches: (1) printf-style format specifiers like %s, %d, %02d, %1$s; (2) escaped characters like \n, \r, \t, \a, \b, \f, \v, and \r\n; (3) Java MessageFormat placeholders like {0}, {1}.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for rules to be applied

processingNotes: If the file has a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark, the corresponding encoding (e.g. UTF-8, UTF-16) is used; otherwise the default encoding specified in the filter options is used.
If the output encoding is UTF-8 and the input was also UTF-8, a BOM is used in the output only if one was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is used in the output.
The type of line-breaks in the output is the same as the one of the original input.
Java properties can also be represented in XML; to process such documents, use the XML Filter or the XML Stream Filter instead.
limitations: None known.

examples.title: Basic properties extraction
examples.description: Simple properties file with comments. Both key=value and key:value separators are supported.
examples.input:
# Example of Java properties

labelOK= OK
msgBadFile: Invalid input file
examples.output: Two text units extracted: 'OK' (name=labelOK) and 'Invalid input file' (name=msgBadFile)

examples.title: Key condition filtering
examples.description: Using key condition with pattern '.*text.*' and extractOnlyMatchingKey=true to extract only entries whose keys contain 'text'.
examples.input:
key1 = Text for key1
text.err1 = Text for text.err1
menu_text_file = Text for menu_text_file
examples.output: Only 'Text for text.err1' (key=text.err1) and 'Text for menu_text_file' (key=menu_text_file) are extracted. 'key1' does not match the pattern and is skipped.

okf_rainbowkit.overview: The Rainbow Translation Kit Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for any of the packages generated by the Rainbow Translation Kit Creation Step. This is a read-only filter — it can read translation kit packages but cannot re-write them. It processes packages by reading the manifest.rkm file, which describes the directory layout and content of the translation kit.

general.openManifest.description: When enabled, the manifest file is opened (and can possibly be modified) before processing it. This allows you, for example, to merge back only a sub-set of the files listed in the manifest rather than all of them. See the Rainbow Translation Kit Manifest documentation for details on the manifest format and what modifications are possible.
notes: Disabling this option skips the manifest review step and processes the kit directly without user intervention.
The 'noprompt' configuration preset sets this to false to allow non-interactive processing.

limitations: This is a READ-ONLY filter. It does not generate an output corresponding to the input and must be used for input only.
processingNotes: Rainbow translation kits come in different types of packages, usually corresponding to a directory with a specific layout and content.
The file called manifest.rkm within the kit directory is the file to use as the input file to process the whole package.

okf_regex.overview: The Regex Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for any type of text-based formats where text can be captured using regular expressions. You define rules with regular expressions that indicate what part of the document to process, with each rule associated with an action telling the filter what to do with the different capturing groups. The filter can work with any text-based document, making it highly flexible for custom and proprietary formats such as SRT subtitles, Mac .strings files, and line-based or block-based text files.

extraction.startString.description: The character(s) specifying the start of a string. Entering several characters defines several ways to start a string. For example, if both single and double quotes can start strings, enter both characters. The position of each start character must correspond to the position of its matching end character in the endString parameter.
notes: If you define multiple beginning characters, you must define an equal number of end characters with matching positions.

extraction.endString.description: The character(s) specifying the end of a string. If you have defined several beginning characters in startString, you must define an equal number of end characters, and the position of each end character must correspond to the position of its corresponding beginning character.

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of characters, with positions corresponding to each other

extraction.regexOptions.description: Bitfield of Java regex options applied to all rules. Common values: 8 = MULTILINE (^ and $ match line terminators), 32 = DOTALL (dot matches line-feed), 40 = MULTILINE + DOTALL, 2 = CASE_INSENSITIVE. These options apply globally to all rules in the configuration. To override an option for a specific rule, use the (?idmsux-idmsux) construct within that rule's pattern.
notes: These options are global to all rules. Use the (?idmsux-idmsux) inline flag construct within a rule's pattern to override options for that specific rule.

extraction.extractOuterStrings.description: Set this option to extract any translatable strings found outside the scope of the defined extraction rules. When enabled, text between the configured startString and endString delimiters that falls outside rule matches will also be extracted as translatable content.
notes: This option is not yet fully implemented according to the filter's known limitations.

extraction.oneLevelGroups.description: When enabled, automatically closes any opened section (START_GROUP) when a new one starts. This allows defining only start-of-section rules without requiring corresponding end-of-section rules. When disabled, each 'Start a section' action must have a corresponding 'End a section' action.

extraction.mimeType.description: The MIME type value to use when extracting content with these parameters. The value is used to identify the type of document and may also change the way text is written back into the original format. Most of the time 'text/plain' should be fine.

output.localizeOutside.description: Set this option to extract any translatable item that is not within the scope of a localization directive. Selecting to extract or not outside localization directives allows you to mark up fewer parts of the source document.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this option to take effect

output.useBSlashEscape.description: Set this option if the way to escape a character in the format is to use a back-slash prefix (e.g. \" to represent a literal double-quote inside a string). When enabled, the filter will correctly handle backslash-escaped characters during extraction and output.

output.removeBSlashEscape.description: When enabled, removes backslash escape sequences from the extracted content. This is useful when the source format uses backslash escaping but you want the extracted translatable text to contain the unescaped characters.

output.useDoubleCharEscape.description: Set this option if the way to escape a character in the format is to double it (e.g. "" to represent a literal double-quote inside a string). This is an alternative to backslash escaping used by some formats.

output.useLd.description: Set this option to enable the filter to recognize localization directives when they are present. If this option is not set, any localization directive in the input file will be ignored.

subfilters.subFilter.description: Specifies a sub-filter configuration ID to apply to the extracted content. When set, the extracted text units will be further processed by the specified sub-filter, enabling nested content extraction.

inlineCodes.description: Configuration for detecting and converting parts of extracted text into inline codes. When the 'Has inline codes' option is set on a rule, patterns defined here are used to identify portions of the translatable text that should be treated as non-translatable inline codes (e.g., HTML tags, placeholders, format specifiers).

limitations: The whole document is loaded in memory to apply the regular expressions, which may cause issues with very large documents.
The option 'Extract strings outside the rules' (extractOuterStrings) is not yet implemented.
processingNotes: Input encoding is determined by Unicode BOM if present; otherwise the default encoding specified when opening the document is used.
The filter does not recognize any encoding declarations in the document and therefore cannot update them.
If the output encoding is UTF-8 and the input was also UTF-8, a BOM is used only if one was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is used.
Line-break type in the output matches the original input.

Parsing proceeds sequentially: the filter searches for the first matching rule from the current position, applies its action, moves the position to the end of the match, and repeats until no more matches are found.
Rules are evaluated in list order — the first rule that matches at any given position wins.

examples.title: Key-value extraction with regex groups
examples.description: Extracting translatable text and identifiers from a simple key=value format using capturing groups. The regex captures the key (group 1) and value (group 3), with group 1 assigned as the identifier and group 3 as the source text.
examples.input:
[ID1]=Text for ID1
[ID2]:Text for ID2

Regex: ^\[(.*?)](=|:)(.*?)$
Action: Extract the content of the source group
Source group: 3, Identifier group: 1
examples.output:
<trans-unit id="1" resname="ID1" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en">Text for ID1</source>
</trans-unit>
<trans-unit id="2" resname="ID2" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en">Text for ID2</source>
</trans-unit>

examples.title: SRT subtitle configuration
examples.description: Configuration for extracting translatable text from SRT (Sub-Rip Text) subtitle files. Uses DOTALL+MULTILINE regex options (40), captures timestamp as a note (group 1) and subtitle text as source (group 2), with metadata rules for extracting start and end display times.
examples.input:
Rule expression: ^(\d+:\d+:\d+.*?)\n(.*?)(\n\n+|\z)
regexOptions: 40 (DOTALL + MULTILINE)
Source group: 2, Note group: 1
preserveWS: true, useCodeFinder: true

Metadata rules:
  subtitle_start_display: \d+:\S+(?=\s+-->)
  subtitle_end_display: (?<=-->\s{1,50})\d+:\S+

examples.title: Mac .strings file configuration
examples.description: Configuration for Macintosh .strings files with multiple rules to handle different patterns: comments followed by quoted keys, comments followed by unquoted keys, standalone strings, block comments, and line comments. Includes code finder rules for format specifiers, escape sequences, and HTML tags.
examples.input:
Rule 0 (Comments+String): /\*(.*?)\*/\n"(.*?)"\s*=(.*?(?<!\\)(?:\\{2})*");
Source group: 3, Name group: 2, Note group: 1

Code finder rules:
  %(([-0+#]?)[-0+#]?)((\d\$)?)(([\d\*]*)(\.[\d\*]*)?)...  (format specifiers)
  (\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v  (escape sequences)
  \<(/?)\w+[^>]*?>  (HTML tags)

examples.title: FPRM-only metadata rules
examples.description: Metadata rules that are only accessible in the FPRM configuration file (not in the UI). Rule name and matched regex content is added as metadata to the TextUnit.

okf_sdlpackage.overview: The SDL Trados Package Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for SDL Trados SDLPPX and SDLRPX files. It is an extension of the Archive Filter that reads the input package, detects SDLXLIFF files for the specified language pair, and uses the XLIFF Filter (with the okf_xliff-sdl configuration) to process the content. Each SDLXLIFF file inside the package corresponds to a sub-document in the Okapi filter events.

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling settings. When enabled, uses regex-based pattern matching to detect inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within translatable text. Includes options for merging adjacent codes and moving boundary codes to skeleton.

identification.description: File identification settings that control which files inside the package are processed and which sub-filters are used to handle them.

identification.fileNames.description: Comma-separated file name patterns (glob-style) used to identify which files inside the SDL package should be extracted and processed. Default patterns match TMX, XLF, and XLFF files (*.tmx,*.xlf,*.xlff).

identification.mimeType.description: MIME type assigned to the filter's container format. Defaults to 'application/x-archive'.

identification.configIds.description: Comma-separated list of Okapi filter configuration IDs corresponding to each file name pattern. Each config ID specifies which sub-filter to use for processing matching files. The order must match the fileNames patterns. Default value 'okf_tmx,okf_xliff,okf_xliff' maps TMX files to the TMX filter and XLF/XLFF files to the XLIFF filter. The SDLXLIFF files are typically processed using the okf_xliff-sdl configuration of the XLIFF Filter.

limitations: Reading an SDLPPX file and writing it back produces another SDLPPX file, not an SDLRPX file.
Subject to all limitations of the XLIFF Filter, which is used internally to process the SDLXLIFF content.
processingNotes: This filter is an extension of the Archive Filter.
It reads the input package, detects the SDLXLIFF files for the specified language pair, and uses the XLIFF Filter (with the okf_xliff-sdl configuration) to process the content.
Each SDLXLIFF file inside the package corresponds to a sub-document in the Okapi filter events.

okf_simplification.overview: The Simplification Filter is a wrapper around another (internal) filter, used to transform events generated by that internal filter through resource simplification. It internally creates and employs two steps — Resource Simplifier Step and Inline Codes Simplifier Step — either or both of which can be activated through the filter parameters. The default internal filter configuration is okf_xmlstream (XML Stream Filter), but any registered Okapi filter configuration can be used.

filterConfigurationId.description: The configuration ID for the internal filter. The internal filter is instantiated automatically and generates events that are then simplified based on the simplifyResources and simplifyCodes parameters. The default value is 'okf_xmlstream', which uses the XML Stream Filter. Any filter configuration for any filter registered with Okapi can be used.

simplifyResources.description: When enabled, simplifies resources generated by the internal filter by removing references from resources (resource flattening).
notes: At least one of simplifyResources or simplifyCodes must be active, otherwise the filter generates an error.

dependsOn.condition: At least one of simplifyResources or simplifyCodes must be enabled

simplifyCodes.description: When enabled, merges adjacent inline codes in the source part of a text unit, and moves leading and trailing codes of the source to the skeleton.
notes: At least one of simplifyResources or simplifyCodes must be active, otherwise the filter generates an error.

dependsOn.condition: At least one of simplifyResources or simplifyCodes must be enabled

limitations: This filter is BETA.
processingNotes: The filter internally creates and employs two steps: Resource Simplifier Step and Inline Codes Simplifier Step.
The default configuration for the internal filter is okf_xmlstream (XML Stream Filter).
Any filter configuration for any filter registered with Okapi can be used as the internal filter.

okf_table.overview: The Table Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for plain text table documents such as CSV, TSV, and fixed-width column files. It supports multiple table types including comma-separated values, tab-separated values, and fixed-width columns, with configurable column definitions for source, target, comment, and ID columns. The filter handles text qualifiers, escaping modes, trimming, inline codes, and multi-line text units.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation in extracted text. When inline codes are detected (via code finder rules or other mechanisms), these rules control how the codes are simplified for presentation to translators.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: If the file has a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark, the corresponding encoding (e.g. UTF-8, UTF-16) is used. Otherwise, the default encoding specified in filter options is used.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is included only if the input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is written.
The type of line-breaks in the output matches the original input line-breaks.

examples.title: CSV with qualified fields containing delimiters
examples.description: When text qualifiers are used, field delimiters inside qualified fields do not split the field. For example, with comma as field delimiter and quotation mark as qualifier, the value ["Field, containing comma"] is treated as a single field.
examples.output: Field, containing comma (with qualifiers removed if 'Exclude qualifiers' is enabled)

examples.title: CSV escaping with duplicate qualifier
examples.description: When a field contains the active text qualifier character, escaping by duplication preserves the qualifier within the content.
examples.output: Text, "quoted text"

examples.title: CSV escaping with backslash
examples.description: When backslash escaping mode is used, qualifiers within fields are escaped with a backslash prefix.
examples.output: Text, "quoted text"

examples.title: Trimming modes for qualified vs non-qualified fields
examples.description: When trimming is set to 'Only entries without qualifiers', qualified fields keep their whitespace while non-qualified fields are trimmed. When set to 'All', both types are trimmed.
examples.output: Mode 'Only non-qualified': [  text ] and [non-qualified]. Mode 'All': [text] and [non-qualified].

okf_tmx.overview: The TMX Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for TMX (Translation Memory eXchange) documents. TMX is a standard that defines a file format for transporting translation memory data from one translation tool to another. The filter processes TMX 1.4b documents, supporting multiple target languages, segmentation control, and inline code detection.

extraction.processAllTargets.description: Set this option to read all target <tuv> elements into the text unit. When disabled, only the selected target is read and all remaining ones become part of the skeleton. Any effect this setting has depends on the following pipeline steps and the ability they have to process multiple targets.
notes: Any effect this setting has depends on the following pipeline steps and the ability they have to process multiple targets.

extraction.consolidateDpSkeleton.description: Set this option to consolidate the skeleton parts and send fewer events through the pipeline. This is sufficient in most cases, but as a pipeline developer sometimes you might want to have access to more fine-grained resources in the pipeline.

extraction.exitOnInvalid.description: By default invalid <tu>s are skipped along with warning message(s). By using the default setting or ignoring the warning messages you might run the risk of getting a processed file that doesn't match the input file. Enable this option if you want to be notified immediately of invalid content and want to correct the file before re-running it.
notes: Warning: By using the default (false) setting or ignoring the warning messages you might run the risk of getting a processed file that doesn't match the input file.

extraction.segType.description: Controls whether a segment is created for each extracted <tu> entry, based on the segtype attribute. The following integer values are available: 'Always creates the segment' — Creates the segment regardless of what the value of the segtype attribute is. 'Never creates the segment' — Never creates the segment, even if the segtype attribute is set to 'sentence'. 'Creates the segment if segtype is sentence or is undefined' — Creates the segment when the segtype attribute is set to 'sentence' or if it is not defined. 'Creates the segment only if segtype is sentence' — Creates the segment only if the segtype attribute is set to 'sentence'.

output.escapeGT.description: Set this option to have all greater-than characters ('>') escaped as '>' in the output.

extraction.propValueSep.description: This string will be used to separate duplicate property values when there are duplicate properties. Default is ', '.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) in translatable text segments.

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text. Each rule defines a pattern that matches inline code sequences.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder to simplify the translatable text.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable portion), keeping translatable text cleaner.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation to reduce complexity for translators.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

limitations: The <sub> element is not supported. When such element is found, a warning is issued, and the element content is put with the content of its parent element.
The filter is not able to reconstruct any DTD declaration.
processingNotes: If the document has an encoding declaration it is used; otherwise, UTF-8 is used as the default encoding regardless of the actual default encoding specified when opening the document.

If the output encoding is UTF-8 and the input encoding was also UTF-8, a Byte-Order-Mark is used for the output only if one was detected in the input. If the input encoding was not UTF-8, no BOM is used.
The type of line-breaks of the output is the same as the one of the original input.

okf_tradosrtf.overview: The Trados-Tagged RTF Filter implements the IFilter interface for RTF files prepared with the Trados translation layer of styles. It reads Trados-Tagged RTF documents where special styles are associated to different parts of the content and segmentation markers are present. This is a READ-ONLY filter — it cannot merge back extracted data. A Trados-Tagged RTF differs from a normal Trados-translated RTF in that formatting information uses Trados styles (tw4wInternal, tw4wExternal) rather than true RTF codes.
limitations: This is a READ-ONLY filter. It does not generate an output corresponding to the input and must be used for input only.
The filter only understands inline codes represented with Trados styles (tw4wInternal, tw4wExternal), not RTF with normal formatting.
processingNotes: The document encoding is determined from font encoding information declared in the RTF document. If no default encoding is declared, the user-specified encoding is used as the default.
Only text marked by Trados segmentation markers is extracted. Any text outside the segment markers is ignored and considered non-translatable.

okf_transifex.overview: The Transifex Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface to process online Transifex projects. Transifex is an open-source translation collaborative platform where one party can upload files for translation, and another party can access these files to translate them online or locally. The filter takes a local Transifex project file (.txp) as input, connects to the Transifex host to retrieve PO resources, and processes them as standard filter events. When used with a filter-writer, modified PO files are pushed back to the Transifex project.

general.openProject.description: Set this option to open the project file (.txp), and possibly modify it, before processing it. This allows you to select which resource you want to process. When enabled, a dialog box is presented where you can configure the project ID, host, credentials, and select which resources to include in processing. When disabled, the filter processes the .txp file directly without prompting.
notes: The default configuration (okf_transifex) has this set to true, while the alternate configuration (okf_transifex-noPrompt) sets it to false for non-interactive processing.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within the translatable text extracted from the PO resources downloaded from Transifex.

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text extracted from Transifex PO resources.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable).

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

limitations: This filter is BETA.

Transifex loses fuzzy flags and the corresponding translations when downloading a PO file that was uploaded with fuzzy entries. The filter avoids labelling as fuzzy to prevent losing target text during round-trips, which may result in resources marked as 100% translated in Transifex even when translations are not final.
Only PO-based files in Transifex are handled, not TS files.

Due to limitations in the pipeline mechanism, this filter cannot be used with steps that re-write different output corresponding to the resource, other than the Filter Events to Raw Document Step. For example, it will not work properly with the Rainbow Translation Kit Creation Step.
processingNotes: The filter takes a local .txp project file as input. If the file contains no resources, the filter connects to the Transifex host using the credentials specified in the file and fetches the list of all resources. If the file already contains resources, the list is not refreshed.

For each PO resource in the project whose source language matches the specified source locale, the filter downloads the translation file for the given target language. If no translation file exists, an empty one is created.
Downloaded PO files are opened and processed, sending standard filter events as received from a PO file. When one resource is done, it moves to the next.
If the filter-writer is used (e.g., in the Filter Events to Raw Document Step), modified PO files are recreated and pushed back into the Transifex project using the same credentials.
The .txp project file must be UTF-8 encoded. Lines with only spaces/tabs and lines starting with # are ignored.
Source and target locale values in the .txp file are used only as fall-back values; in most situations the locales are driven by the application calling the filter.

examples.title: Transifex project file format
examples.description: Example .txp project file showing the format for configuring a Transifex project connection, including host, credentials, locale settings, and resource selection. Each resource can optionally have a tab-separated yes/no flag to indicate whether it should be processed (defaults to yes if omitted).
examples.input:
host=http://www.transifex.net
user=johndoe
password=xrt34@asf
projectId=myproject
protectApproved=yes
# Fall-back values for the source and target locales:
sourceLocale=en
sourceLocale=fr
# Resources
myfile1xmlpo	yes
myfile2odtpo	no

okf_ts.overview: The TS Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for Qt TS (Translation Source) resource files. It is implemented in the class net.sf.okapi.filters.ts.TsFilter. The implementation is based on the TS specification from Qt/Trolltech for the Linguist TS file format.

output.escapeGT.description: No specific wiki documentation for this parameter. Controls whether the greater-than character (>) is escaped in the output as >.

output.decodeByteValues.description: When enabled, converts all <byte value="[value]"/> elements in the TS document to raw characters. If this option is not set, the <byte/> elements will not be converted and will be treated as inline codes. Note that those characters are stored as special elements in TS because they are invalid in XML, so it may be better to keep them as inline codes if you are working with other XML-based formats like XLIFF or TMX.
notes: Note: Characters stored as <byte/> elements are invalid in XML. Converting them to raw characters may cause issues when working with XML-based formats like XLIFF or TMX.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Set this option to use the specified regular expressions on the text of the extracted items. Any match will be converted to an inline code. By default the inline code detection expression covers printf-style format specifiers (e.g., %d, %s, %1$d), C-style escape sequences (e.g., \n, \t, \r\n), and numbered placeholders (e.g., {0}, {1}).

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regex patterns used to identify inline codes in translatable text. The default expression matches three categories: (1) printf-style format specifiers like %d, %s, %1$d with optional flags and width/precision; (2) C-style escape sequences including \r\n, \a, \b, \f, \n, \r, \t, \v; and (3) curly-brace numbered placeholders like {0}, {1}.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable content).

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

limitations: There are sometimes issues with extracting and merging back <numerusform> elements.
Do a round-trip before sending for translation to verify correct extraction and merging.
processingNotes: The type of line-breaks of the output is the same as the one of the original input.

The <translation> element's type attribute is mapped as follows: no type attribute means the entry is extracted with target approved='yes'; type='unfinished' means approved='no'; type='obsolete' means the entry is NOT extracted (these are unused entries, not just obsolete translations).
The filter stores <translatorcomment> elements as text unit Property.TRANSNOTE, which can be used by subsequent steps or editors.

examples.title: Default inline code patterns
examples.description: The default regular expression patterns used for inline code detection in TS files, covering printf-style format specifiers, C-style escape sequences, and numbered placeholders.

okf_ttx.overview: The TTX Filter implements the IFilter interface for Trados TTX documents. TTX is an XML-based bilingual format used by some versions of the Trados tools and supported by several other tools. The filter handles extraction of source and target text from TTX files, with configurable segmentation modes to control how pre-segmented and unsegmented content is processed. There are no official public specifications available for the TTX format.

inlineCodes.description: Controls inline code detection and handling within translatable text segments. When enabled, regex patterns are used to identify inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) in the extracted text. Sub-options allow merging adjacent codes into single placeholders and moving boundary codes to the skeleton.

mode.description: Processing mode settings that control how the filter extracts content from TTX files, particularly how it handles segmentation.

mode.segmentMode.description: Controls the extraction mode based on possible existing segments in the TTX file. Value 0 (Auto-detect): The filter scans the file for at least one existing segment. If one is found, only text in existing segments is extracted (equivalent to mode 1). If no segment is detected, all text is extracted (equivalent to mode 2). Value 1 (Extract only existing segments): Only text within existing <Tu>/<Tuv> segments is extracted. If the file is not pre-segmented, no text is extracted at all. Value 2 (Extract all): Extracts all text whether it is in existing segments or not. In this mode, unsegmented text parts in <Raw> elements are also extracted alongside segmented parts, potentially combining them into a single text unit with multiple segments.
notes: Segmentation is often a cause for interoperability issues. For better compatibility with the tool that created the TTX files, it is recommended to work with pre-segmented documents.
TTX is a format where the target text cannot be represented if the text is not segmented. The output of the filter includes any new <Tu> and <Tuv> elements needed.

output.description: Settings controlling how the translated TTX output document is written.

output.escapeGT.description: When enabled, all greater-than characters ('>') are escaped as '>' in the output document. By default (false), greater-than characters are left unescaped.

limitations: The TTX element <df> may cause problems in some cases, for example when spanning across an external tag. For instance, when the TTX file contains the extraction of HTML code where a <df> element for bold goes across paragraph boundaries which are external tags in TTX. Those cases should be rare.
processingNotes: If the document has an encoding declaration it is used; otherwise UTF-8 is used as the default encoding, regardless of the actual default encoding specified when opening the document.
If the output encoding is UTF-8 and the input was also UTF-8, a Byte-Order-Mark is used only if one was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is used.
The type of line-breaks of the output is the same as the one of the original input.
TTX is a format where target text cannot be represented if the text is not segmented, so the filter output includes any new <Tu> and <Tuv> elements needed.

Segmented entries with translations are extracted with both source and target text. Match types are assigned based on MatchPercent: above 100 with Origin=xtranslate → EXACT_LOCAL_CONTEXT, above 99 → EXACT, between 1 and 99 → FUZZY. The Origin attribute value is carried over to the annotation when present.

examples.title: Extract all mode (mode 2) — full text extraction
examples.description: In mode 2 (Extract all), both segmented and unsegmented text parts within <Raw> elements are extracted as a single text unit with multiple segments.
examples.output: [Part 1 ][Part 2][ Part 3]

examples.title: Extract existing segments only (mode 1)
examples.description: In mode 1 (Extract only existing segments), only text within pre-existing <Tu>/<Tuv> segments is extracted. Unsegmented text parts are ignored.
examples.output: [Part 2]

okf_txml.overview: The TXML Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for Wordfast Pro TXML documents. TXML is a proprietary XML-based bilingual format used by Wordfast Pro and supported by several other tools. There are no official public specifications available for the format.

output.allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Controls whether empty translations are written out as-is in the output document. When enabled (true), empty target segments remain empty in the output. When disabled (false), a copy of the source text is used in place of any empty translation, effectively filling in untranslated segments with their source content.
notes: The default configuration 'okf_txml' has this set to true (empty targets allowed). The alternate configuration 'okf_txml-fillEmptyTargets' sets this to false so that empty targets are filled with source text on output.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within translatable text segments.

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regex patterns used to identify inline codes in translatable text. Each rule contains a regex pattern that matches inline code sequences within segment content.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: When enabled, consecutive inline codes that appear next to each other are merged into a single placeholder, reducing clutter in the translatable text.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: When enabled, inline codes that appear at the beginning or end of a segment are moved to the skeleton (non-translatable portion), keeping the translatable text cleaner.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation in translatable text.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

limitations: The TXML format does not allow different source and target content for the part between segments. If any of those inter-segment parts change after extraction, the difference cannot be represented when merging — only the source representation is preserved.
processingNotes: Input encoding is determined from the document's encoding declaration if present; otherwise UTF-8 is used as default regardless of any specified default encoding.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is included only if the input was UTF-8 and had a BOM. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is added.
If the original document had an XML encoding declaration it is updated on output; if it did not have one, a declaration is automatically added.
Line-break type in the output matches the original input document.
TXML files are organized into blocks of one or more segments. Each block is extracted as a single text unit with as many segments as the block contains.
Existing translations are extracted along with the source. Segments labeled with gtmt="true" and not modified="true" are also set as AltTranslationsAnnotation annotations.

Only the latest translation revision is extracted; translations in <revisions> elements are ignored. The filter does not create revision entries — updated translations overwrite previous ones without creating a revision record.

okf_vignette.overview: The Vignette Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for export/import XML documents used with the Vignette Content Management System. The Vignette XML documents are the files generated by the vgnexport tool of Vignette. It extracts translatable content from specific named attribute elements within the export XML structure, supporting both bilingual and monolingual processing modes.

mode.description: Processing mode settings controlling how the filter handles the Vignette export document, including monolingual vs bilingual processing and CDATA handling.

mode.monolingual.description: Set this option to process monolingual documents, where all importContentInstance elements are to be extracted. When this option is set, the filter does not use the source ID and the locale ID parameters. When disabled (the default), the filter operates in bilingual mode, requiring matching source and target importContentInstance blocks identified by the source ID.
notes: When monolingual mode is enabled, the constraints about having matching source and target importContentInstance elements do not apply.

mode.useCDATA.description: Controls whether the filter expects and uses CDATA sections in the Vignette export XML. When enabled (the default), content is expected inside CDATA sections. When disabled, content is treated as escaped HTML.

mode.quoteMode.description: Quote handling mode for the filter. Controls how quotes are processed in the content.

mode.quoteModeDefined.description: Indicates whether the quote mode has been explicitly defined. When true, the quoteMode value is used; when false, default quote handling applies.

identification.description: Content identification settings that control which elements are extracted and how source/target blocks are matched.

identification.partsNames.description: Enter the list of the values for the name attribute of the <attribute> elements to extract. The list must be comma-separated. Each entry corresponds to a named content part in the Vignette export XML. For example, if the content body is stored in the 'MYVCM-BODY' entry, in the XML export it appears as: <attribute name="MYVCM-BODY"><valueCLOB>data</valueCLOB></attribute>.
notes: The number of entries must match the number of entries in the partsConfigurations list, and they must be in the same order.

identification.partsConfigurations.description: Enter the list of the filter configurations to use for each extractable part. This list must be comma-separated. It must contain the same number of entries as the list of the elements to extract (partsNames), and be in the same order. Use 'default' to indicate no specific filter. Other values are Okapi filter configuration IDs such as 'okf_html' for parts containing HTML content.
notes: Must contain the same number of entries as partsNames and in the same order.

dependsOn.condition: must have matching number of comma-separated entries in the same order

identification.sourceId.description: Enter the value for the name attribute of the <attribute> element that contains the source ID for a given block. This is used to match source and target importContentInstance blocks — both blocks must share the same source ID value. Not used when monolingual mode is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be false (only used in bilingual mode)

identification.localeId.description: Enter the value for the name attribute of the <attribute> element that contains the locale ID for a given block. Not used when monolingual mode is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be false (only used in bilingual mode)

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling settings. Allows pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within translatable text extracted from Vignette content.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within the extracted translatable text.

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text. Each rule defines a pattern that, when matched, marks the matching text as an inline code (non-translatable placeholder).

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder to simplify the translatable segments.

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable portion), keeping only meaningful translatable content in the segment.

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation in the extracted segments.

limitations: Very large documents may cause memory problems.
processingNotes: Input and output encodings are forced to UTF-8.

You must have an importContentInstance element for both the source and the target language in the export file to be able to extract data. Entries with only a source importContentInstance will not be extracted but will be listed as a warning. Entries with no source importContentInstance will also not be extracted and listed as a warning.
The relationship between source and target importContentInstance blocks is established via the source ID parameter — both blocks must share the same source ID value.
These bilingual constraints do not apply when monolingual mode is used.

examples.title: Vignette XML attribute element structure
examples.description: Shows how content is stored in a Vignette export XML file. The extractable content is identified by the name attribute of the attribute element.
examples.output: The text 'data' inside the valueCLOB element is extracted as a translatable text unit.

examples.title: Default configuration with CDATA
examples.description: The default configuration extracts common Vignette content parts (SMCCONTENT-TITLE, SMCCONTENT-BODY, etc.) with CDATA sections enabled. Parts containing HTML use okf_html sub-filter; others use 'default'.
examples.input:
partsNames=SMCCONTENT-TITLE, SMCCONTENT-ABSTRACT, SMCCONTENT-BODY, SMCCONTENT-ALT
partsConfigurations=default, okf_html, okf_html, default
useCDATA=true
monolingual=false
examples.output: Extracts SMCCONTENT-TITLE and SMCCONTENT-ALT as plain text, SMCCONTENT-ABSTRACT and SMCCONTENT-BODY through the okf_html sub-filter for HTML-aware extraction.

examples.title: Escaped HTML configuration (no CDATA)
examples.description: Alternative configuration for Vignette files that do not use CDATA sections. The same parts are extracted but useCDATA is set to false and quoteMode is explicitly defined.
examples.input:
partsNames=SMCCONTENT-TITLE, SMCCONTENT-ABSTRACT, SMCCONTENT-BODY
partsConfigurations=default, okf_html, okf_html
useCDATA=false
quoteModeDefined=true
quoteMode=0
examples.output: Content is treated as escaped HTML rather than CDATA-wrapped content. Quote handling uses mode 0 (no special quote processing).

okf_wiki.overview: The Wiki Filter is an Okapi component for extracting translatable text from wiki markup. Currently the only supported style of markup is Dokuwiki. It extracts translatable content from headers, paragraphs, list items, image captions, and table cells/headers.

preserve_whitespace.description: When set to true, prevents the filter from collapsing whitespace in the extracted content. When false (the default), the filter collapses whitespace normally.
notes: Set via YAML configuration as 'preserve_whitespace: true'

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation. All Dokuwiki syntax as described in the Dokuwiki syntax documentation is supported as inline codes. Custom inline codes can be defined using the 'custom_codes' YAML configuration with either a single 'pattern' for placeholder tags, or a 'start_pattern' and 'end_pattern' pair for opening/closing tag pairs. Patterns are specified as regular expressions that match non-zero-width runs of text, and matches are turned into inline codes.
notes: Custom codes are defined in YAML using 'custom_codes' with entries containing either 'pattern' for placeholders or 'start_pattern'/'end_pattern' for paired tags
Patterns must be valid regular expressions matching non-zero-width text runs

path.description: Path to an external YAML configuration file for the filter parameters.

limitations: Attributes of inline codes (link and image URLs, etc.) are not exposed for translation or special processing.
Embedded HTML, PHP, etc., is not extracted.
processingNotes: If the file has a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark, the corresponding encoding (e.g. UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.) is used. Otherwise, the input encoding used is the default encoding that was specified when opening the document.
All Dokuwiki syntax is supported as inline codes.

examples.title: Dokuwiki markup extraction
examples.description: Shows which parts of typical Dokuwiki markup are extracted as translatable text. Headers, paragraphs, list items, image captions, and table cells/headers are all extracted.
examples.input:
=== Header ===
Paragraph
 * List item
  * List item

{{image.jpg|Image caption}}

^ Table header 1 ^ Table header 2 |
| Table cell 1 | Table cell 2 |
examples.output: Extracted text units: 'Header', 'Paragraph', 'List item' (x2), 'Image caption', 'Table header 1', 'Table header 2', 'Table cell 1', 'Table cell 2'

examples.title: Custom inline codes configuration
examples.description: Shows how to define custom inline codes in YAML configuration using regex patterns. A single 'pattern' creates placeholder tags, while 'start_pattern'/'end_pattern' creates paired opening/closing tags.

okf_wsxzpackage.overview: The WSXZ Package Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for WSXZ (Worldserver TKIT) files. It is an extension of the Archive Filter that reads input packages, detects SDLXLIFF files for the specified language pair, and uses the XLIFF Filter (with the okf_xliff-sdl configuration) to process the content. Each SDLXLIFF file inside the package corresponds to a sub-document in the Okapi filter events.
limitations: TMX and TBX files are ignored.
Subject to all limitations of the XLIFF Filter.
processingNotes: This filter is an extension of the Archive Filter.
It detects SDLXLIFF files for the specified language pair within the input package.
Uses the XLIFF Filter with the okf_xliff-sdl configuration to process content.
Each SDLXLIFF file inside the package corresponds to a sub-document in the Okapi filter events.

okf_xliff.overview: The XLIFF Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for XLIFF 1.2 (XML Localisation Interchange File Format) documents. XLIFF is an OASIS Standard that defines a file format for transporting translatable text and localization-related information across a chain of translation and localization tools. The filter supports standard XLIFF as well as SDL XLIFF and IWS XLIFF variants, with configurable segmentation, alt-trans handling, and ITS annotation support.

extraction.preserveSpaceByDefault.description: When enabled, whitespace is preserved by default in source and target content. When disabled, if a <trans-unit> element has xml:space="preserve", white spaces inside source and target are left as-is; if xml:space is not present or has a value different from "preserve", the content is unwrapped.

extraction.includeExtensions.description: Set this option to include non-standard information (e.g. match types) in added <alt-trans> elements. When enabled, extra information beyond the XLIFF standard is preserved in alt-trans output.

extraction.includeIts.description: When enabled, ITS and ITSXLF annotations are included during extraction. These annotations carry localization metadata such as translate, terminology, and localization note information.

extraction.inlineCdata.description: When enabled, CDATA sections encountered in source/target content are treated as inline codes rather than as text content.

extraction.subAsTextUnit.description: When enabled, <sub> elements found inside <bpt>, <ept>, <ph>, and <it> elements are processed as separate text units instead of being included in the code of the parent inline element.

extraction.ignoreInputSegmentation.description: Set this option to ignore any segmentation information contained in the input XLIFF. When this option is set, all segmented content is reduced to new unsegmented content when extracted. Note that any <alt-trans> data attached to a given segment is also lost when this option is enabled.
notes: Any <alt-trans> data attached to a given segment is also lost when this option is enabled.

extraction.skipNoMrkSegSource.description: When enabled, <seg-source> elements that do not contain <mrk mtype='seg'> marker elements are skipped. If the content of <seg-source> has no explicit <mrk mtype='seg'> element, it is normally treated as if the whole content is a single segment; this option skips such entries instead.

extraction.fallbackToID.description: Select this option to use the value of the id attribute of the <trans-unit> element as a fall-back value for the text unit name if resname is not present. This may be useful for XLIFF documents that use resname-like values for id but do not bother providing resname (as they should).

extraction.forceUniqueIds.description: When enabled, the filter forces all text unit IDs to be unique. This is useful when processing XLIFF documents that may contain duplicate trans-unit id values across different file elements.

states.targetStateMode.description: Controls how the target state attribute is handled in the output. Valid values: 0 = do not modify; 1 = set to a specific value; 2 = set only if not already present. The specific state value is controlled by the targetStateValue parameter.

states.targetStateValue.description: The state value to set on target elements when targetStateMode is configured to set a state value. Common values include 'needs-translation', 'translated', 'final', etc. as defined by the XLIFF 1.2 specification.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to a non-zero value for this to take effect

states.useTranslationTargetState.description: When enabled, translation-specific target state handling is used. This applies the configured target state value specifically when translations are provided.

states.editAltTrans.description: Set this option to allow <alt-trans> elements that exist in the input to be modified in the output. The existing entries will be treated like added ones, allowing their content to be updated during processing.

states.addAltTrans.description: Set this option to allow the addition of new <alt-trans> elements in the output. For example, when this option is set and the Leveraging Step is applied to a given XLIFF input document, its output includes the translation matches possibly found during leveraging.

states.addAltTransGMode.description: Set this option to use the <g>/<x> notation for inline codes (instead of the <bpt>/<ept>/<ph> notation) in the added <alt-trans> elements. Only applies when addAltTrans is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this to take effect

output.addTargetLanguage.description: Set this option to add the target-language attribute in <file> if it is not present. When enabled, the filter ensures that each <file> element in the output includes a target-language attribute.

output.overrideTargetLanguage.description: Set this option to override the language of the target set in the input XLIFF. When enabled, the value of the target-language attribute and the value of xml:lang in all <target> elements are set to the target language specified by the user, regardless of their original values.
