This is useful when using an XLIFF document as a template for several outputs in different target languages.
notes: Depending on what the original XLIFF document contains, this option may result in outputs where existing <alt-trans> elements do not correspond to the target language of a given <trans-unit> anymore.

output.allowEmptyTargets.description: Set this option to prevent copying of source text into an empty target. When enabled, empty <target> elements are allowed in the output XLIFF document rather than being populated with source text.

output.alwaysAddTargets.description: When enabled, target elements are always added to trans-units in the output, even if no translation is available. Without this option, target elements are only added when translations exist.

output.alwaysUseSegSource.description: When enabled, the <seg-source> element is always used in the output, even when the content consists of a single unsegmented segment.

output.outputSegmentationType.description: Select the type of segmentation representation to use for the output. Values: 0 = 'Segment only if the input text unit is segmented' — each text unit uses <seg-source> only if the original was segmented; 1 = 'Always segment' — all text units use <seg-source>, even if the original was not segmented or is a single segment; 2 = 'Never segment' — no text units are segmented, all <seg-source> elements are removed; 3 = 'Segment only if segmented, regardless of input' — only text units made of more than one part are segmented, regardless of input.

parser.useCustomParser.description: Set this option to use an XML stream parser different from the default one. The default parser for this filter is the Woodstox XML parser. The reason for this is that the Java parser that comes with most VMs implements the collapsing of whitespace characters using a recursive method that can cause a stack overflow error in XLIFF documents with large chunks of element content (e.g. in SDLXLIFF files).

parser.factoryClass.description: The name of the factory class that will instantiate the custom XML stream parser to use. For example: com.ctc.wstx.stax.WstxInputFactory. Only used when useCustomParser is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

sdl.useSdlXliffWriter.description: When enabled, the SDL-specific XLIFF writer is used for output. This writer handles SDL-specific metadata and formatting conventions found in SDLXLIFF files.

sdl.useSegsForSdlProps.description: When enabled, SDL properties (locked, conf, origin) are read from and written to segments rather than the TextContainer level. From M35 on, the three properties are stored at the segment level with correct values, but those segment-level properties are read-only.

sdl.sdlSegConfValue.description: The SDL segment confirmation value to set on output segments. Common values include 'Translated', 'Draft', 'ApprovedTranslation', etc.

iws.useIwsXliffWriter.description: When enabled, the Idiom WorldServer-specific XLIFF writer is used for output. This writer handles IWS-specific metadata and formatting conventions.

iws.iwsBlockFinished.description: When enabled, segments with a 'finished' translation status in IWS XLIFF are blocked from further modification.

iws.iwsBlockLockStatus.description: When enabled, segments with a locked status in IWS XLIFF are blocked from modification.

iws.iwsBlockMultipleExact.description: When enabled, segments with multiple exact TM matches in IWS XLIFF are blocked from modification.

dependsOn.condition: should be true to include the multiple exact matches that this option blocks

iws.iwsBlockTmScore.description: When enabled, segments with a TM score at or above the threshold specified by iwsBlockTmScoreValue are blocked from modification.

iws.iwsBlockTmScoreValue.description: The TM score threshold value for blocking segments. Segments with a TM match score at or above this value are blocked when iwsBlockTmScore is enabled. Default is 100.00.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this threshold to be applied

iws.iwsIncludeMultipleExact.description: When enabled, multiple exact TM matches are included in the IWS XLIFF output rather than being reduced to a single match.

iws.iwsRemoveTmOrigin.description: When enabled, TM origin metadata is removed from the IWS XLIFF output.

iws.iwsTransStatusValue.description: The translation status value to set on IWS XLIFF segments. Common values include 'finished', 'pending', etc.

iws.iwsTransTypeValue.description: The translation type value to set on IWS XLIFF segments. Common values include 'manual_translation', 'machine_translation', etc.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: When enabled, the code finder is used to detect inline codes in translatable text based on regular expression patterns defined in the rules. This allows identification of placeholders, tags, and other non-translatable patterns within text content.

xtmAttributes.description: When enabled, XTM-specific attributes are processed and preserved in the XLIFF output.

limitations: The content of the <sub> element is currently not supported as text. Any element found inside <bpt>, <ept>, <ph>, and <it> (including <sub>) is included in the code of the parent inline element. A warning is generated when a <sub> element is detected.

The special marker <mrk mtype='protected'> is supported by converting content into an inline code. If <mrk mtype='x-its-translate-yes'> is used within such a marker, it is not supported and its content is placed into the original data part of the inline code.

The pre-defined configuration okf_xliff-sdl provides additional support for SDLXLIFF files. SDL properties (locked, conf, origin) are stored in the TextContainer of the target rather than in each segment; if there are several segments, the values are those of the last segment. From M35 on, segment-level properties are also stored with correct values but are read-only.
processingNotes: If the document has an encoding declaration it is used; otherwise UTF-8 is used as the default encoding regardless of any specified default.
If input and output encoding are both UTF-8, a BOM is used in output only if one was detected in the input. If input was not UTF-8, no BOM is used in the UTF-8 output.
Line-break type in the output matches the original input.
If a <trans-unit> has xml:space="preserve", whitespace in source/target is preserved. Otherwise, content is unwrapped.
The <seg-source> segmentation is only carried over if its content (without segment markers) matches the <source> element content.
Alt-trans entries are sorted based on match types and score. The match-quality attribute is used as score if it is an integer or percentage; otherwise score is set to 0.
The default parser is Woodstox because the standard Java XML parser can cause stack overflow errors in XLIFF documents with large element content (e.g. SDLXLIFF files).

examples.title: Alt-trans mapping
examples.description: How <alt-trans> elements are mapped to Okapi AltTranslationsAnnotation. The match-quality attribute is used as score (integer or percentage), origin attribute maps to annotation origin (defaults to 'SourceDoc'), and entries are sorted by match type and score. Entries with score above 99 are typed as EXACT, above 0 as FUZZY.
examples.output: AltTranslationsAnnotation with score=95, origin='MyTM', matchType=FUZZY

examples.title: Segmentation in seg-source
examples.description: How <seg-source> segmentation is handled. The segmentation is carried over only if the content of <seg-source> without markers matches <source>. If no explicit <mrk mtype='seg'> elements exist, the whole content is treated as a single segment.
examples.input:
<trans-unit id="1">
  <source>First sentence. Second sentence.</source>
  <seg-source>
    <mrk mtype="seg" mid="1">First sentence.</mrk>
    <mrk mtype="seg" mid="2">Second sentence.</mrk>
  </seg-source>
</trans-unit>
examples.output: Two segments extracted: 'First sentence.' and 'Second sentence.'

examples.title: Output segmentation types
examples.description: The four output segmentation modes control how segmentation is represented in output: (0) preserve input segmentation, (1) always add seg-source, (2) never segment/remove all seg-source, (3) segment only multi-part entries regardless of input.

okf_xliff2.overview: The XLIFF-2 Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for XLIFF 2.x (XML Localisation Interchange File Format) documents. XLIFF v2 is an OASIS Standard that defines a file format for transporting translatable text and localization-related information across a chain of translation and localization tools. The filter provides basic support for XLIFF 2.x core features including extended attributes/namespaces, segments and ignorables, inline codes, notes, groups, and the XLIFF 2.x Metadata Module.

extraction.needsSegmentation.description: When enabled, resegments any unit that is marked with canSegment="yes". Units with an existing target cannot be segmented. This controls whether the filter applies segmentation to content during extraction.
notes: Units with an existing target cannot be segmented even when this option is enabled.

extraction.forceUniqueIds.description: Forces text unit IDs to be unique within units. When enabled, ensures that tag IDs do not collide within the same unit.

extraction.simplifyTags.description: Simplifies inline tags during extraction, reducing complex tag structures to simpler representations.

extraction.ignoreTagTypeMatch.description: When enabled, ignores tag type mismatch between source and target elements. Normally the filter validates that tag types in the target match those in the source.

states.discardInvalidTargets.description: When enabled, discards invalid target elements rather than rejecting the entire file. This allows processing to continue even when some target elements do not conform to the expected format.

states.subfilterOverwriteTarget.description: Controls whether the sub-filter always overwrites the target content. When enabled, the sub-filter output replaces any existing target content.

output.writeOriginalData.description: When enabled, the output includes original data when available. Controls whether the filter writes originalData attributes in the output XLIFF document.

output.maxValidation.description: When enabled, ensures the XLIFF-2 parser used by the filter performs the maximum verification of the format during parsing. This provides stricter validation of the input document structure and content.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Enables pattern-based detection of inline codes using the configured code finder rules. When enabled, the filter will scan text content for patterns matching the defined regular expressions and treat matches as inline codes.

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regex patterns used for inline code detection. Each rule defines a regular expression pattern that identifies inline codes within text content.

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: When enabled, merges consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder, simplifying the tag structure for translation.

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: When enabled, moves inline codes that appear at segment boundaries (leading or trailing) to the skeleton, removing them from the translatable content.

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation. Defines how complex inline code structures should be reduced to simpler forms.

limitations: Skeleton not supported.
Comments are lost in the merged document.
Original XML formatting lost in merged document.
Attributes can be reordered.
Attributes may be removed/added compared to the original depending on the default values and logic in the XLIFF 2 Toolkit.
processingNotes: Provides basic support for XLIFF 2.x core including: extended attributes and namespaces, segments and ignorables, inline codes, notes, groups, and XLIFF 2.x Metadata Module.

okf_xml.overview: The XML Filter processes XML documents using a DOM-based parser with full ITS (Internationalization Tag Set) support for W3C ITS 1.0 and 2.0 specifications. It determines translatable content, inline elements, and other localization-related aspects through ITS rules, either embedded in the document or specified via an external parameters file. Because it uses DOM parsing and loads the entire file into memory, very large XML documents may be better served by the XML Stream Filter. The filter's parameters are stored in an XML file (an ITS document) that can include both standard ITS rules and Okapi-specific filter options using the okapi-framework:xmlfilter-options namespace.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation in extracted content. Defines how inline codes within translatable text are simplified for cleaner segmentation and processing.

path.description: Path to an external ITS rules file (.fprm) that defines the filter's parameters. The parameters file is an ITS document that can contain standard ITS rules (translateRule, withinTextRule, locNoteRule, etc.) as well as Okapi-specific filter options using the okapi-framework:xmlfilter-options namespace. By default (without a parameters file), the filter uses ITS defaults: all element content is translatable and no attribute values are translatable. The parameters file can override these defaults and configure additional options such as lineBreakAsCode, codeFinder, omitXMLDeclaration, escapeQuotes, escapeGT, escapeNbsp, extractIfOnlyCodes, inlineCdata, and extractUntranslatable.
notes: Filter options (okp:options, okp:codeFinder) must be placed in the parameters file (.fprm), not in embedded or linked ITS rules. Options placed in embedded or linked ITS rules have no effect.
When multiple options are used, they must be set in a single <okp:options> element.
The parameters file uses the ITS XML format and supports ITS extensions via the namespace http://www.w3.org/2008/12/its-extensions (idValue, whiteSpaces).

limitations: In some cases, the ITS rule withinTextRule with the value 'nested' may act like it has a value 'yes' instead.
In output, the values of xml:lang attributes are not updated to reflect the target language.
The whole input file is loaded into memory during extraction, which may cause memory limitations for very large documents.
processingNotes: Input encoding: Uses the document's encoding declaration if present; otherwise defaults to UTF-8 regardless of any specified default encoding.
Output BOM handling: If output encoding is UTF-8 and input was also UTF-8, a BOM is written only if one was detected in the input. If input was not UTF-8, no BOM is written.
If the original document had an XML encoding declaration it is updated in the output; if it did not, one is automatically added.
Line-breaks in output match the type used in the original input document.
DOM-based parser loads the entire document into memory; for very large files without ITS needs, consider the XML Stream Filter instead.

examples.title: ITS rules for RESX files
examples.description: Parameters file defining ITS rules for processing Microsoft RESX documents. Demonstrates translateRule selectors, idValue for resource names, localization notes, and codeFinder for inline codes.
examples.input:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<its:rules xmlns:its="http://www.w3.org/2005/11/its"
 xmlns:itsx="http://www.w3.org/2008/12/its-extensions"
 xmlns:okp="okapi-framework:xmlfilter-options"
 its:translate="no" version="1.0">
 <its:translateRule selector="/root" translate="no"/>
 <its:translateRule selector="//data[not(@type) and not(starts-with(@name, '>'))]/value" translate="yes" itsx:idValue="../@name"/>
 <its:translateRule selector="//data[@mimetype]/value" translate="no"/>
 <its:locNoteRule locNoteType="description"
  locNotePointer="../comment" selector="//data[not(@type) and not(starts-with(@name, '>') or starts-with(@name, '$'))]/value"/>
 <okp:codeFinder useCodeFinder="yes">#v1
count.i=2
rule0=(\{[^}]+?\})
rule1=<(/?)\w+[^>]*?>
 </okp:codeFinder>
</its:rules>

examples.title: Using idValue for resource names
examples.description:
Demonstrates how the itsx:idValue extension attribute defines XPath expressions to generate text unit resource names from element attributes. The @name attribute value becomes the resource name for each

element.
examples.input: text 1
examples.output: Text unit with resource name 'id1' containing 'text 1'. Note: xml:id has precedence over idValue if both are present.

examples.title: Compound idValue using concat()
examples.description: Shows how to build unique IDs from multiple attributes using XPath string functions when multiple translatable elements share a parent ID.
examples.input:
<doc>
 <its:rules version="1.0" xmlns:its="http://www.w3.org/2005/11/its"
  xmlns:itsx="http://www.w3.org/2008/12/its-extensions">
  <its:translateRule selector="//text" translate="yes" itsx:idValue="concat(../@name, '_t')"/>
  <its:translateRule selector="//desc" translate="yes" itsx:idValue="concat(../@name, '_d')"/>
 </its:rules>
 <msg name="id1">
  <text>Value of text</text>
  <desc>Value of desc</desc>
 </msg>
</doc>
examples.output: Two text units: 'id1_t' for <text> content and 'id1_d' for <desc> content.

examples.title: Filter options: lineBreakAsCode and escapeQuotes
examples.description:
Shows how to configure multiple filter options in a single okp:options element. lineBreakAsCode treats line-breaks (e.g. 
) as inline codes, escapeQuotes controls double-quote escaping in element content, and escapeGT controls greater-than escaping.

examples.title: codeFinder for inline code detection
examples.description: Defines regular expressions to capture text spans that should be treated as inline codes. Rule0 matches XML-type tags, rule1 matches variables enclosed in # or numbers enclosed in %.
examples.input:
<its:rules version="1.0"
 xmlns:its="http://www.w3.org/2005/11/its"
 xmlns:okp="okapi-framework:xmlfilter-options">
 <okp:codeFinder useCodeFinder="yes">#v1
count.i=2
rule0=<(/?)
+[^>]*?>
rule1=(#\w+?\#)|(%\d+?%)
 </okp:codeFinder>
</its:rules>
examples.output:
Matches like , ,
are treated as inline codes from rule0. Matches like #VAR# and %1% are treated as inline codes from rule1.

examples.title: extractUntranslatable with selective exclusion
examples.description: Extracts untranslatable content for context purposes while excluding specific elements using the localeFilterList ITS attribute workaround.
examples.output: Untranslatable contents are extracted but marked as translate='no' in XLIFF, except elements matching the localeFilterRule which are excluded entirely.

examples.title: whiteSpaces extension for preserving spaces
examples.description: Uses the itsx:whiteSpaces extension to globally apply space preservation to specific elements, equivalent to xml:space='preserve'. Note: xml:space has precedence over whiteSpaces if both are present.
examples.input: Some txt with    many spaces.
examples.output: The <pre> element content is extracted with all spaces, tabs, and line breaks preserved.

okf_xmlstream.overview: The XML Stream Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for XML documents. It uses a stream parser, which allows processing of much larger documents than a DOM-based parser like the XML Filter. It uses the same YAML-based configuration syntax as the HTML Filter, with most of the same parameters available. If ITS (Internationalization Tag Set) support is needed, the XML Filter should be used instead.

parser.description: Controls parser behavior including well-formedness assumptions and whitespace handling. Contains sub-properties for assumeWellformed and preserveWhitespace.

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling configuration. Enables pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.) within translatable text. Supports regex patterns for code detection, merging adjacent codes, moving boundary codes to skeleton, and simplifier rules. Same behavior as described in the HTML Filter documentation.

attributes.description: Global attribute extraction rules that map attribute names (e.g. 'xml:lang', 'xml:id', 'xml:space') to their rule configuration. These rules apply across all elements in the document. Each attribute rule specifies a ruleType (ATTRIBUTE_TRANS, ATTRIBUTE_WRITABLE, ATTRIBUTE_READONLY, ATTRIBUTE_ID, ATTRIBUTE_PRESERVE_WHITESPACE) and can optionally be scoped to specific elements via allElementsExcept or onlyTheseElements, and can include conditions. The default configuration handles xml:lang as writable, xml:id and id as ID attributes, and xml:space for whitespace preservation control.
notes: All parameters described in the HTML Filter's documentation are also available in the XML Stream Filter, with the exception of escapeCharacters.

editorTitle.description: The title displayed in the parameters editor UI. Defaults to 'Parameters Editor'.

taggedConfig.description: Tagged configuration object for storing additional filter configuration metadata.

path.description: Path to the YAML configuration file for this filter instance.

limitations: There is no transparent support for namespace prefixes: element names must be declared with their prefix in the configuration.
The filter is not case-sensitive — elements like <elem> and <Elem> are treated as identical, which does not conform to the XML specification where element names are case-sensitive.
processingNotes: Input encoding: If the document has an encoding declaration it is used; otherwise UTF-8 is used as default (regardless of any default encoding specified when opening the document).
Output encoding (UTF-8): A BOM is written only if the input was also UTF-8 and a BOM was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is written.
If the original document had an XML encoding declaration it is updated in the output; if it did not have one, an encoding declaration is automatically added.
Line-breaks in the output preserve the same type as the original input.

Quote/apostrophe escaping can be configured via quoteModeDefined and quoteMode parameters: UNESCAPED=0 (no escaping), ALL=1 (named entities for both), NUMERIC_SINGLE_QUOTES=2 (named for double, numeric for single), DOUBLE_QUOTES_ONLY=3 (escape double quotes only).

examples.title: CDATA subfilter for HTML content
examples.description: Configure the filter to process CDATA sections containing HTML through an additional HTML filter. The global_cdata_subfilter parameter specifies the filter configuration to apply to all CDATA content.

examples.title: PCDATA subfilter for escaped HTML content
examples.description: Configure the filter to process double-escaped HTML content in PCDATA (element content). The element containing such content must match a TEXTUNIT rule — content matched by INCLUDE rules will not be passed to the subfilter.

examples.title: Double-escaped HTML in XML
examples.description: Example of XML content with double-escaped HTML that requires PCDATA subfiltering. The HTML entities are escaped an additional time within the XML element content.
examples.input:
<test>
  &lt;p&gt;This is embedded HTML content within a p tag.&lt;/p&gt;
</test>

examples.title: Quote mode configuration
examples.description: Configure escaping behavior for quote and apostrophe characters in the output. Mode 3 escapes only double quotes.

examples.title: Default XML Stream configuration
examples.description: The default configuration assumes well-formed XML, does not preserve whitespace globally, and sets up standard XML attribute handling for xml:lang (writable), xml:id/id (ID), and xml:space (whitespace preservation).
examples.input:
assumeWellformed: true
preserve_whitespace: false

attributes:
  'xml:lang':
    ruleTypes: [ATTRIBUTE_WRITABLE]
  'xml:id':
    ruleTypes: [ATTRIBUTE_ID]
  'id':
    ruleTypes: [ATTRIBUTE_ID]
  'xml:space':
    ruleTypes: [ATTRIBUTE_PRESERVE_WHITESPACE]
    preserve: ['xml:space', EQUALS, preserve]
    default: ['xml:space', EQUALS, default]

okf_yaml.overview: The YAML Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for YAML files and supports Ruby on Rails message variables. It extracts translatable string values from YAML key-value pairs, arrays, and maps, assigning each extracted entry a name based on the full path of its parent identifiers (e.g., fr/activerecord/errors/messages/exclusion). The filter is implemented in the class net.sf.okapi.filters.yaml.YamlFilter.

extraction.extractAll.description: When enabled, extracts all strings that have a key associated. When disabled, no strings with associated keys are extracted. If a regular expression for exceptions (excludeKeys) is defined, the behavior is inverted for keys matching that expression: when extractAll is true, matching keys are excluded; when extractAll is false, matching keys are included.
notes: Works in conjunction with excludeKeys — the exceptions regex inverts the default extraction behavior for matching keys.

extraction.includeIsolatedStrings.description: Set this option to extract strings that are not associated directly to a key value. These are stand-alone string values such as items in YAML arrays or sequences that exist without a key-value mapping.

extraction.excludeKeys.description: A regular expression that corresponds to the keys that should have a behavior inverse to the default behavior selected for key/string pairs. When extractAll is true, keys matching this expression are NOT extracted. When extractAll is false, keys matching this expression ARE extracted. If useFullPath is enabled, the regular expression applies to the full key path rather than just the immediate key name.

dependsOn.condition: determines whether matching keys are excluded (when true) or included (when false)
when true, exception regular expressions apply to the full key path

keys.useAsName.description: Use the value of the YAML key as the name (resname) of the extracted item. In XLIFF output, this corresponds to the resname attribute. For example, in a YAML structure like 'messages: exclusion: "text"', the resname would be set to 'exclusion' (or the full path if useFullPath is enabled).

keys.useFullPath.description: Use the full nested key path as the name (resname) instead of just the immediate key. For example: 'menu/value/popup/menuitem/value'. The useAsName option must be enabled for this option to take effect. When enabled, exception regular expressions (excludeKeys) also apply to the full path rather than just the immediate key.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for useFullPath to take effect

output.escapeNonAscii.description: When enabled, non-ASCII characters in the output are escaped using YAML escape sequences.

output.wrap.description: Controls line wrapping in the output. Note that wrapped lines in the original source document will be unwrapped in the target document regardless of this setting — all text will be placed on a single line during processing.

output.subFilterProcessLiteralAsBlock.description: When enabled, YAML literal block scalars (using the | indicator) are processed with the configured sub-filter. A sub-filter (e.g., okf_html) can be specified to process the content of all translatable text with that filter.

inlineCodes.enabled.description: Set this option to use the specified regular expressions on the text of extracted items. Any match will be converted to an inline code. The default expressions match printf-style format specifiers (e.g., %d, %s), common escape sequences (\n, \r, \t, etc.), and curly-brace placeholders (e.g., {0}, {1}).
notes: This option cannot be used together with the sub-filtering option.

inlineCodes.rules.description: Regular expression patterns used to detect inline codes in extracted text. Each pattern match is converted to an inline code placeholder. The default patterns are: (1) printf-style format specifiers like %d, %s, %0.2f; (2) escape sequences like \n, \r, \t, \a, \b, \f, \v; (3) curly-brace placeholders like {0}, {1}. The default expression is: ((%(([-0+#]?)[-0+#]?)((\d\$)?)(([-\d*]*)(\.[-\d*]*)?)[dioxXucsfeEgGpn])|((\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v)|(\{\d.*?\}))

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: When enabled, consecutive inline codes that appear next to each other are merged into a single placeholder, reducing clutter in the translatable segment.

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: When enabled, inline codes that appear at the beginning or end of a segment are moved to the skeleton (non-translatable portion), keeping the translatable text cleaner.

inlineCodes.simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying the representation of inline codes in extracted segments.

limitations: Wrapped lines in the original source document will be unwrapped in the target document — all text will be on a single line.

If an illegal character is introduced during translation, the user will need to manually add double or single quotes so that the target YAML file is valid. A WARNING will be issued by the filter if this scenario can be detected.
processingNotes: If the file has a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark, the corresponding encoding (e.g., UTF-8, UTF-16) is used. Otherwise, the default encoding specified when opening the document is used.
The type of line-breaks in the output is the same as the one in the original input.
Each extracted entry is assigned a name based on the sequence of all its parent identifiers. For example, a value under fr/activerecord/errors/messages/exclusion gets that full path as its name.

examples.title: Ruby on Rails YAML extraction
examples.description: Shows which parts of a typical Ruby on Rails i18n YAML file are extracted for translation. All string values in key-value pairs, arrays, and maps are extractable.
examples.input:
fr:
  activerecord:
    errors:
      template:
        header:
          list: [one, two, three]
          map: {key: value, key2: value2}
          one: "Impossible d'enregistrer {{model}}: 1 erreur"
          other: "Impossible d'enregistrer {{model}}: {{count}} erreurs."
        body: "Veuillez vérifier les champs suivants :"
      messages:
        inclusion: "n'est pas inclus(e) dans la liste"
        exclusion: "n'est pas disponible"
        invalid: "n'est pas valide"
        confirmation: "ne concorde pas avec la confirmation"
examples.output: Extracted text units: 'one', 'two', 'three' (list items), 'value', 'value2' (map values), and all quoted string values in key-value pairs.

examples.title: Text unit naming with full key path
examples.description: Demonstrates how extracted text units are named using the full path of parent identifiers. The text 'n'est pas disponible' gets the name 'fr/activerecord/errors/messages/exclusion'.
examples.input:
fr:
  activerecord:
    errors:
      messages:
        exclusion: "n'est pas disponible"
examples.output:
Text unit name: fr/activerecord/errors/messages/exclusion
Text unit content: n'est pas disponible

examples.title: Default inline code patterns
examples.description: The default code finder expressions detect printf-style format specifiers, escape sequences, and curly-brace placeholders as inline codes.
examples.output: Matches: %d, %s, %0.2f (format specifiers); \n, \r, \t (escape sequences); {0}, {1} (placeholders)

b-o-m-conversion.overview: The BOM Conversion step adds or removes the Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) to or from UTF-8 and UTF-16 input files. The Byte-Order-Mark is a special Unicode mark used to indicate if a file is in little-endian (U+FFFE) or big-endian (U+FEFF) mode. Takes raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output.

removeBOM.description: When true, removes the Byte-Order-Mark from input files if one is detected. By default, only the BOM of UTF-8 files are removed. When false (the default), the step adds a BOM to input files if one is not already present. Note that when adding BOMs, the input files must already be in UTF-8 or UTF-16, and the encoding of each file must be specified so the utility can add the proper type of BOM.
notes: When adding BOMs (removeBOM=false), you must specify the encoding of each file so the utility can add the proper type of BOM.

alsoNonUTF8.description: When true, also removes the BOM from UTF-16 files if one is detected. This option only applies when removing BOMs. Removing the BOM from UTF-16 files is not recommended, as UTF-16 files must have a BOM.
notes: Removing the BOM from UTF-16 files is not recommended: UTF-16 files must have a BOM.

dependsOn.condition: must be true (only applies when removing BOMs)

limitations: Only UTF-8 and UTF-16 files are currently supported, not UTF-32.
processingNotes: The step operates on raw documents, reading and rewriting the file with or without the BOM.
When adding BOMs, the encoding of each file must be specified so the correct BOM type (little-endian or big-endian) can be added.
UTF-16 files require a BOM to indicate byte order; removing it is not recommended.

examples.title: Remove UTF-8 BOM
examples.description: Configure the step to remove the BOM from UTF-8 files only (the default removal behavior). Set removeBOM to true and leave alsoNonUTF8 as false.
examples.output: UTF-8 files will have their BOM removed; UTF-16 files are left unchanged.

examples.title: Remove BOM from all Unicode files
examples.description: Configure the step to remove the BOM from both UTF-8 and UTF-16 files. Note that removing BOM from UTF-16 is not recommended.
examples.output: Both UTF-8 and UTF-16 files will have their BOM removed.

examples.title: Add BOM to files
examples.description: Configure the step to add a BOM to input files that don't already have one. The encoding of each file must be specified separately so the correct BOM type is added.
examples.output: Files without a BOM will have the appropriate BOM prepended based on their encoding.

batch-translation.overview: This step creates a translation memory from text units extracted from a raw document, using an external tool to provide the translation. It extracts content via the document's associated filter, creates a temporary HTML document with source segments, invokes an external translation tool via a user-defined command line, and then aligns source and translated entries into a Pensieve TM and/or TMX document. Takes: Raw document. Sends: Raw document (or optionally the generated TMX document).

command.description: The command line used to invoke the external translation tool. The command must take the temporary HTML document named ${inputPath} and generate an output document in the same format named ${outputPath}. The temporary HTML document contains one paragraph per source segment (or per source text unit if not segmented). Available variables in the command line: ${inputPath} (full path of input document), ${inputURI} (URI of input document), ${outputPath} (full path of output document), ${outputURI} (URI of output document), ${srcLangName} (English name of source language, e.g. 'German' for 'de-ch'), ${trgLangName} (English name of target language, e.g. 'Japanese' for 'ja-jp'), ${rootDir} (root directory for the project/batch; in Rainbow this is the parameters folder), ${inputRootDir} (root directory for input files). Additionally, all locale-related variables are available (${srcLang}, ${trgLang}, ${srcLoc}, ${trgLoc}, etc.).
notes: The external tool must create the translated HTML output at the path corresponding to ${outputPath} or ${outputURI}.

blockSize.description: The maximum number of text units that should be passed at the same time to the external tool. This allows processing of very large input documents even with external tools that can only handle small documents. Each input document can be processed using one or more temporary HTML files based on this block size.

origin.description: An optional string that identifies the origin of the translation. The given string is output as a property of the translated entry named 'Origin'. For example, in TMX output it will be generated as <prop type="Txt::Origin">myText</prop>, where myText is the given string.

markAsMT.description: When enabled, marks the TM entries generated as the result of machine translation. For example, when this option is set, the creationId attribute of the target in the generated output is set to 'MT!'.

segment.description: When enabled, segments the extracted text units using SRX rules before sending them to the temporary HTML document. If set, each paragraph of the HTML document will be a sentence. If not set, each paragraph will be an un-segmented paragraph.
notes: Note: Only entries processed by the external tool are placed in the TMX output. Entries that already exist in the TM being populated or in the existing TM are not copied into the TMX output.

srxPath.description: Full path to the segmentation rules file in SRX format that should be used to segment the text units. You can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} in the path, as well as any of the locale variables.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

makeTM.description: When enabled, imports the translated entries into a given Pensieve TM. The entries added to the TM are indexed at the end of each input document, so other steps down the pipeline can only access entries generated from previous documents for a given document.

tmDirectory.description: The directory of the Pensieve TM where to import the entries. If the TM does not exist it will be created. If the TM already exists, entries will be added to the existing TM. You can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} in the path, as well as any of the locale variables.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

makeTMX.description: When enabled, creates a TMX document with the translated entries. A single TMX file is created for all input documents. The file is not generated until the end of the last document, and therefore cannot be used by other steps down the pipeline.

tmxPath.description: Full path of the TMX document to generate. If the file already exists it will be overwritten. You can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} in the path, as well as any of the locale variables.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

sendTMX.description: When enabled, sends the generated TMX document as the raw document to the next step, instead of the original input document.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

checkExistingTm.description: When enabled, looks up each entry that may be sent for translation in an existing Pensieve TM. This allows sending only entries for which no existing translation exists. Existing entries are not re-processed and are not placed in the optional TMX output.

existingTm.description: The directory of the Pensieve TM to look up for existing entries. You can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} in the path, as well as any of the locale variables.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The step extracts content from the raw document using its associated filter and places it in a temporary HTML document where each paragraph corresponds to a source segment (or source text unit if not segmented).
Each input document can be processed using one or more temporary HTML files based on the block size, allowing tools with size limitations to translate very large documents.
Entries added to the Pensieve TM are indexed at the end of each input document, so downstream steps can only access entries generated from previous documents.
The TMX file is not generated until the end of the last document and therefore cannot be used by downstream steps.
The step sends the input raw document unmodified to the next step, unless 'Send the TMX document to the next step' is enabled.

examples.title: Apertium translation on Linux
examples.description: Uses the open-source Apertium machine translation program under Linux to translate the temporary HTML document.

examples.title: ProMT translation on Windows
examples.description: Uses the commercial ProMT application under Windows to translate the temporary HTML document.

char-listing.overview: This step generates a list of all characters used in the translatable text of a given set of documents. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. It is useful when you need to create a font containing only the characters actually used in text (e.g., Japanese bitmap characters for game interfaces), allowing you to reduce font size by including only necessary characters. Text units marked as not translatable are ignored.

outputPath.description: Full path of the result file. The result file is a tab-delimited text file with three columns: the Unicode code point (e.g., U+2192), the character representation (e.g., '→'), and the number of occurrences found for that character. The output list is not in any specific order.

autoOpen.description: When enabled, the result file is automatically opened after the process is complete.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Text units that are set as not translatable are ignored.
The result file is a tab-delimited text file with three columns: Unicode code point, character representation, and occurrence count.
The output list is not in any specific order.

examples.title: Result file output format
examples.description: Example of the tab-delimited result file generated by the step, showing Unicode code point, character representation, and occurrence count columns.
examples.input: Source documents containing translatable text with various characters.

character-count.overview: This step performs character counts on the different parts of a set of documents. It takes filter events and sends filter events. The character counts are saved as annotations that can be used in other steps, such as the Scoping Report Step which can generate a report from the counts. The character count generated follows the GMX-V 2.0 standard.

countInBatch.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per batch. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregated count at the batch level.

countInBatchItems.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per batch item. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregated count at the batch item level.

countInDocuments.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per document. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregated count at the document level.

countInSubDocuments.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per sub-document. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregated count at the sub-document level.

countInGroups.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per group. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregated count at the group level.

bufferSize.description: Size of text buffer used for character counting. A value of 0 indicates the default buffer size.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Character count annotations are always set on each text unit, regardless of the aggregation-level options selected.
The character count follows the GMX-V 2.0 standard (http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html).
Count annotations can be consumed by downstream steps such as the Scoping Report Step.

cleanup.overview: This step cleans strings by normalizing quotes, punctuation, and whitespace ready for further processing. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. By default, all whitespace is normalized before any further processing is performed — all multiple space, tab, etc. characters are replaced with a single instance.

normalizeQuotes.description: When enabled, replaces all quotation marks with straight double quotes (") and all apostrophes with single straight quotes ('). This normalization is applied to both source and target text.

checkCharacters.description: When enabled, detects and removes text units that contain common corrupt character strings. This helps clean data that may have encoding issues or unexpected characters.

matchRegexExpressions.description: Mark segments matching default regular expressions for removal. This option is not currently used.
notes: This option is not currently used.

matchUserRegex.description: When enabled, removes text units that contain text matching the user-defined regular expression. This allows custom pattern-based filtering of segments.

pruneTextUnit.description: When enabled, removes text units that have been marked for removal or have no target text. This cleans up segments flagged by other cleanup operations or those lacking translations.

normalizePunctuations.description: When enabled, standardizes spaces before and after certain punctuation marks to English writing style. This normalizes punctuation spacing conventions.

keepCrNlIntact.description: When enabled, keeps CR and NL characters intact so they are not normalized to an ASCII space along with other whitespace characters like spaces and tabs. By default (when disabled), CR and NL characters are normalized to a single space as part of the general whitespace normalization.

limitations: Does not work with Asian or bi-directional languages.
processingNotes: By default, all whitespace is normalized before any further processing is performed; all multiple space, tab, etc. characters are replaced with a single instance.
The step processes both source and target content of text units.
Text units marked for removal by regex matching or corrupt character detection are removed during the pruning phase if pruneTextUnit is enabled.

code-simplifier.overview: This step joins adjacent inline codes in text units, and optionally moves leading and trailing codes from the text unit to the skeleton. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. Only the source codes are affected by this step, so it should be used before any target is created in the text unit.

removeLeadingTrailingCodes.description: When enabled, removes leading and trailing inline codes from the text units and places them into the skeleton. This effectively strips codes that appear at the boundaries of text content and relocates them to the non-translatable skeleton portion. Only works with GenericSkeleton-based content.

mergeCodes.description: When enabled, merges adjacent inline codes in the text units into a single code. This simplifies the inline code structure by combining codes that appear next to each other with no text content between them.

processingNotes: Only the source codes are affected by this step — it should be used before any target is created in the text unit.
Moving leading and trailing codes to the skeleton only works for GenericSkeleton-based content.

codes-removal.overview: This step removes inline codes or the content of inline codes from text units in a document. Inline codes are spans of data that should be left untouched inside translatable text, such as HTML tags like <b> within a paragraph. The step provides three removal modes: removing just the marker, just the content, or both. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output.

stripSource.description: When enabled, applies the selected removal action to the source text of each text unit. Controls whether inline codes in the source content are processed by the step.

stripTarget.description: When enabled, applies the selected removal action to the target text of each text unit. Controls whether inline codes in the target content are processed by the step.

mode.description: Selects the type of data to remove from inline codes. A code is made of two parts: its markers (always present) and its content (optional). For example, an XLIFF inline code like `<bpt id="1"><b></bpt>` has a marker part (`<bpt>...</bpt>`) and a content part (`<b>`). Available modes: (1) Remove code marker, but keep code content — removes the code marker but preserves its content, so the content becomes part of the surrounding text. (2) Remove code content, but keep code marker — removes the code content but preserves its marker, so the inline code still exists but has been generalized and the original format data cannot be retrieved. (3) Remove code marker and code content — removes the whole inline code entirely, leaving no trace of it. The default value of 2 corresponds to 'Remove code marker and code content'.

includeNonTranslatable.description: When enabled, the removal action is applied even to text units marked as non-translatable. If this option is not set, all non-translatable text units are left untouched.

replaceWithSpace.description:
When enabled, replaces codes with spaces where the code type is Code.TYPE_LB or the code's content contains any of the following expressions (case insensitive) or characters:
,
,
, \n, \r, \u0085, \u2028, \u2029. For example, an XLIFF inline code `<br>` representing a line break would be replaced with a space instead of being removed entirely.
notes: This option only applies when the 'Remove code marker and code content' mode is selected.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to 'Remove code marker and code content' (value corresponding to removing both marker and content)

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Inline codes consist of two parts: markers (always present) and content (optional). The step can independently remove either or both parts.
The step processes both source and target text independently based on the stripSource and stripTarget flags.

Line break codes are detected by Code.TYPE_LB type or by matching content patterns:
,
,
, \n, \r, \u0085, \u2028, \u2029 (case insensitive).

examples.title: XLIFF inline code structure
examples.description: Illustrates the marker and content parts of an inline code in XLIFF. The <bpt> and </bpt> tags form the marker part, while <b> is the content part. The removal mode determines which parts are stripped.

convert-segments-to-text-units.overview: Converts segmented text units (paragraphs) into multiple text units, one per aligned sentence pair, for each target locale. This step is useful for preparing input for steps such as the Diff Leverage Step which require un-segmented text units. It accepts filter events and outputs filter events. If a TextUnit resource refers to another IResource or is a referent, it is passed through unchanged even if it has segments.
limitations: Using this step may prevent merging, so it should only be used in pipelines that do not require translated translation kits to be merged.
TextUnit resources that refer to another IResource or are referents are passed through as-is even if they have segments — processing these would require considerable implementation effort.
processingNotes: The step has no configurable parameters — it operates automatically on all segmented text units.
Each aligned segment pair for all target locales is converted into its own complete text unit.
Referent and reference TextUnit resources are passed through unchanged to preserve resource linkage integrity.

examples.title: Preparing input for Diff Leverage Step
examples.description: Use this step before the Diff Leverage Step to convert segmented paragraphs into individual text units with single segments, which is required by Diff Leverage Step.

copy-or-move.overview: The Copy Or Move step copies or moves files from one location to another within an Okapi pipeline. It takes raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output. Files are copied or moved into the location specified in the output settings tabs, with configurable protection methods for handling existing files at the destination.

copyProtectionMethod.description: Controls how existing files at the destination are handled during copy or move operations. Available options: 'overwrite' overwrites any existing files without prompt; 'backup' creates a backup of any existing file before copying/moving; 'skip' skips copying/moving when there is already an existing file in the output location.
notes: This is an enum-style parameter with three possible values: overwrite existing files, backup existing files, or skip copy/move.

move.description: When enabled, files are moved (deleted from the source location after transfer) instead of copied. When disabled (default), files are copied, leaving the originals in place.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Files are copied or moved into the location specified in the output settings tabs.
The step expects raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output.
The step supports OUTPUT_URI, SOURCE_LOCALE, and TARGET_LOCALE parameter mappings for determining output file paths.

create-target.overview: This step creates the target entry for text units, and optionally copies the source content and/or properties into the newly created target. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. It is commonly used in pipelines where a target language container needs to be initialized before translation or other processing steps.

copyContent.description: When enabled, copies the source content into the created target. This is useful when you want the target to start as a copy of the source text, which can then be modified by subsequent translation or transformation steps.

copyProperties.description: When enabled, copies the source properties into the created target. Properties include metadata attached to the text unit source that should also be present on the target side.

overwriteExisting.description: When enabled, copies the source content and/or the source properties to the target even if the target already exists. By default (false), existing target content is preserved and not overwritten.

createOnNonTranslatable.description: When enabled, creates a target entry even if the text unit is marked as non-translatable. This ensures all text units have a target container regardless of their translatable status.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The step processes filter events and handles text units, creating target segments as they pass through the pipeline.
Requires both source and target locale to be set in the pipeline context.

desegmentation.overview: This step joins all the segments of each source and/or target text of each text unit into a single content. It takes filter events and sends filter events. Non-segmented entries and text units flagged as non-translatable are left untouched.

desegmentSource.description: Set this option to desegment the source text of the text units. When enabled, all segments of the source content in each text unit are joined into a single content.

desegmentTarget.description: Set this option to desegment the target of the text units. Only applies if there is a target text present in the text unit.

renumberCodes.description: Set this option to renumber the IDs of each joined entry to match an original set of IDs that is a single sequence of numbers starting at '1'. This option can be used to restore the original IDs in content that was segmented with the 'Renumber code IDs' option in the Segmentation step.
notes: This is designed to work in conjunction with the 'Renumber code IDs' option in the Segmentation step to restore original code IDs after desegmentation.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Non-segmented entries are left untouched.
Text units flagged as non-translatable are left untouched.

diff-leverage.overview: This step compares the source text of two (or three) documents to try to leverage existing translation for new source content. It takes filter events and sends filter events. It supports either a bilingual second input (old source + old translation) or two monolingual inputs (old source and old translation separately), where text units between the second and third documents must be aligned.

fuzzyThreshold.description: Sets the leverage threshold score between 1 and 100. If the result of the comparison process is equal to or above the specified threshold, the old target text is leveraged; otherwise it is ignored. A score of 100 means exact match (both codes and text must match).
notes: A score of 100 means exact match only, requiring both codes and text to match.

codesensitive.description: When enabled, inline codes are taken into consideration when computing the quality of matches between old and new source text. When disabled, only the textual content is compared.

diffOnly.description: When enabled, the step performs a diff comparison only without copying the match or creating a leverage annotation. The TextUnit is simply marked as matched.

copyToTarget.description: When enabled, copies the leveraged translation to (or over) the target. A leverage annotation will still be created regardless of this setting.
notes: WARNING: Copied target will not be segmented and any existing target will be lost.

limitations: The comparison process of the text units is done in memory: you may not be able to process very large documents.
processingNotes: The step requires two or three inputs: (1) the new document to translate, (2) a bilingual document with old source and old translation, OR (2) old source document and (3) old translation document.
When using three documents, the text units between the second and third must be aligned (same number, same order).
The comparison process is performed in memory, which may limit the size of documents that can be processed.

encoding-conversion.overview: This step converts the character set encoding of an input document, and in some cases, updates its encoding declaration. It takes a raw document as input and sends back a raw document. The step can un-escape various character reference notations (NCR, CER, Java-style) during reading, and re-escape characters using a configurable notation during writing. It also automatically detects and updates encoding declarations in XML and HTML documents.

unescapeNCR.description: When enabled, un-escapes all types of numeric character references (NCRs) when reading the input document. For example: `ÿ`, `á` and `&#e1;` will be un-escaped to 'á'. If this option is not set, any NCR in the input document will remain in the exact same form in the output document.

unescapeCER.description: When enabled, un-escapes the standard HTML character entity references (CERs) when reading the input document. For example: `á` will be un-escaped to 'á'. If this option is not set, any CER in the input document will remain in the exact same form in the output document.
notes: The character entity references &, <, >, ' and " are NOT un-escaped as they may need to be preserved regardless of the encoding in HTML and XML documents.

unescapeJava.description: When enabled, un-escapes Java-style escaped characters when reading the input document. For example: `\u00e1` and `\u00E1` will be un-escaped to 'á'. If this option is not set, all Java-style escaped characters in the input document will remain in the exact same form in the output document.

escapeAll.description: Controls which characters should be escaped in the output. When false (default), only characters not supported by the output encoding are escaped. When true, all extended characters are escaped.

escapeNotation.description: Selects the escape notation to use when escaping characters in the output. Integer values map to: 0 = Uppercase hexadecimal NCR (e.g., `á`), 1 = Lowercase hexadecimal NCR (e.g., `á`), 2 = Decimal NCR (e.g., `ÿ`), 3 = Character entity references (e.g., `á`; falls back to uppercase hexadecimal NCR if no entity is defined for the character), 4 = Uppercase Java-style notation (e.g., `\u00E1`), 5 = Lowercase Java-style notation (e.g., `\u00e1`), 6 = User-defined notation (uses the format specified in userFormat).
notes: When using character entity references (value 3), if there is no corresponding entity defined for the character to escape, the uppercase hexadecimal NCR form is used instead.

userFormat.description: A Java format expression that takes an integer as the value to display, used when escapeNotation is set to user-defined (6). For example, the expression `[[%d]]` for 'á' will give `[[255]]`, and the expression `'%x` for 日本語 will give `'65e5'672c'8a9e`. The value displayed is the Unicode code point of the character to escape (unless useBytes is set).

dependsOn.condition: must be set to 6 (user-defined notation)

useBytes.description: When enabled, the values applied to the user-defined format expression are the byte values of the output encoding rather than the Unicode code points. For example, the expression `'%x` for 日本語 will give `'93'fa'96'7b'8c'ea` if this option is set and the output encoding is Shift-JIS, instead of the Unicode code points.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to 6 (user-defined notation)

BOMonUTF8.description: When enabled, adds a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) at the beginning of the output when the output encoding is UTF-8.

reportUnsupported.description: When enabled, lists in the log all characters not supported by the output encoding.

processingNotes: Encoding declarations are automatically updated for XML and HTML documents. The detection algorithm runs on the first 1024 characters of each document.
The encoding declaration detection routine is not XML or HTML aware and cannot distinguish between normal XML/HTML code and XML/HTML comments.

For XML documents: if an XML encoding declaration is detected, the encoding value is updated; if only an XML declaration exists, an encoding declaration is inserted; if the file ends with .xml, an XML declaration with encoding is added at the top.

For HTML documents: if an HTML charset declaration is detected, the charset value is updated; otherwise, if the file extension starts with .htm, a charset declaration is added after the <head> element or inserted with a new <head> element after <html>.
A document can be both an XML and HTML document and have both types of encoding/charset declarations.
limitations: Encoding declaration detection operates on the first 1024 characters only — declarations beyond this point are not updated.
The detection routine is not XML/HTML aware, so encoding declarations inside XML/HTML comments may be incorrectly modified.
Character entity references &, <, >, ' and " are never un-escaped, as they may need to be preserved in HTML and XML documents.

examples.title: User-defined escape format with Unicode code points
examples.description: Using a custom format expression with Unicode code points (useBytes=false). The expression `[[%d]]` for 'á' produces `[[255]]`, and `'%x` for 日本語 produces `'65e5'672c'8a9e`.

examples.title: User-defined escape format with byte values
examples.description: Using a custom format expression with byte values of the output encoding instead of Unicode code points. With Shift-JIS output encoding, the expression `'%x` for 日本語 produces byte-level escapes.
examples.input: escapeNotation: 6, userFormat: "'%x", useBytes: true, output encoding: Shift-JIS
examples.output: 日本語 → '93'fa'96'7b'8c'ea

enrycher.overview: The Enrycher step invokes the Enrycher Web service to provide disambiguation annotations in the content of the source text. It sends groups of source entries to the Enrycher service, gets back ITS annotated results, and maps those results back to Okapi inline annotations that can be used by any other steps. It implements the ITS 2.0 Text Analysis data category for disambiguation.

baseUrl.description: The URL of the Enrycher Web service to use for disambiguation annotations. The default value points to the demo service at http://aidemo.ijs.si/mlw/.

maxEvents.description: The number of events to process at once before sending a query to the Web service. The greater the number, the more segments are sent at the same time to the Web service. This generally improves speed, but the more events are buffered the more memory is required.
notes: Higher values improve speed but increase memory usage.

limitations: This step is BETA.
The Enrycher Web service (as of Aug-18-2013) supports only English and Slovenian.
processingNotes: The amount of annotations added to the content can be very high depending on the type of text.
Events are buffered and sent in batches to the Web service for improved performance.
Results are mapped back to Okapi inline annotations compatible with downstream steps.

external-command.overview: The External Command step runs a given external command on a raw document. It takes a raw document as input and sends a raw document as output. The command line supports variable substitution for input/output paths, source/target locale information, and the project root directory.

command.description: The command-line to execute. The command line must use the input and output paths provided through the variables ${inputPath} and ${outputPath}. Supports the following case-sensitive variables: ${inputPath} (full path of the input document), ${outputPath} (full path of the output document), ${srcLangName} (English name of the language part of the source locale, e.g. 'German' for 'de-ch'), ${trgLangName} (English name of the language part of the target locale, e.g. 'Japanese' for 'ja-jp'), ${srcLang} (language code of the source locale, e.g. 'de' for 'de-ch'), ${trgLang} (language code of the target locale, e.g. 'ja' for 'ja-jp'), ${srcBCP47} (BCP-47 language tag of the source locale, e.g. 'de-CH'), ${trgBCP47} (BCP-47 language tag of the target locale, e.g. 'ja-JP'), ${rootDir} (the root directory for the project/batch; in Rainbow this is the parameters folder).
notes: Variable names are case-sensitive.
${srcBCP47} and ${trgBCP47} are available from M37 onwards.
In Rainbow, ${rootDir} resolves to the parameters folder.

timeout.description: The number of seconds after which the command will be timed out and cancelled. Enter -1 for no timeout.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Takes raw document as input and sends raw document as output.
The step executes the command as an external process and supports timeout-based cancellation.

examples.title: Windows sort command
examples.description: Sorts the input file and creates the corresponding output file using the Windows sort command.

extraction-verification.overview: The Extraction Verification step verifies that an input document can be extracted, merged, then extracted again and produces the same set of events during both extractions. It takes a raw document as input and sends the raw document through unmodified. This verification provides a high level of confidence that the document can be merged safely after translation or other modifications, and is especially useful as the first step in a pipeline that uses newly developed or less tested filters.

stepEnabled.description: Master switch for the step. When set to true, the step performs extraction verification on the input document. When set to false, the input document is simply passed to the next step without any verification.

allEvents.description: When set to true, every event generated by the document is verified between the two extractions. When set to false, only text unit events are verified. Verifying only text units is often a good indicator of whether the merge was done without error, since text units are usually different between two extractions if the merge had problems. Disable this option to save processing time.
notes: Often verifying only text units is a good indicator if the merge was done without error, as text units are usually different between the two extractions if there was a problem.

compareSkeleton.description: When set to true, the skeleton parts of the verified events are also compared. The skeleton is the non-extractable part of the input document.

limit.description: The maximum number of warnings to allow for a single document before moving to the next one. Enter an integer value. Default is 10.

interrupt.description: When set to true, the entire process is stopped when the maximum number of warnings (as defined by the limit parameter) has been reached for a document. When false, processing continues with the next document after reaching the warning limit.

dependsOn.condition: uses the value of limit to determine when to interrupt

limitations: Support for multilingual documents such as XLIFF, TMX, PO, TTX, etc. is BETA.
processingNotes: The step performs a full round-trip: extract → merge → extract again, then compares the events from both extractions to detect discrepancies.

This step is recommended as the first step in pipelines using newly developed or less tested filters, as any error or warning indicates potential problems with merging files back at the end of the process.
The input raw document is passed through unmodified regardless of verification results.

examples.title: Filter development validation pipeline
examples.description: Use the Extraction Verification step as the first step in a pipeline with a newly developed filter to verify round-trip safety before performing translation or other modifications. Any warnings indicate potential merge problems.
examples.input: Place the Extraction Verification step before other processing steps in the pipeline. Enable 'Perform extraction verification' and 'Verify all events' for thorough checking.
examples.output: Warnings are generated for any events that differ between the two extractions. The raw document is passed through unmodified to subsequent steps.

extraction.overview: This step creates a translation package from a set of input documents. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events or raw documents to the next step. The extracted files can be merged back after translation using the Rainbow Translation Kit Merging Step. It supports multiple package formats including Generic XLIFF, PO, OmegaT, Transifex, XLIFF 2.0, and others.

writerClass.description: Selects the type of translation kit to create. The value is the fully-qualified Java class name of the package writer. Available package formats are: Generic XLIFF (files extracted to XLIFF documents), PO Package (files extracted to PO files), Original with RTF (files get an RTF layer), XLIFF with RTF (files extracted to XLIFF then get an RTF layer), OmegaT Project (files extracted to a new OmegaT project), Transifex Project (files uploaded to a new or existing Transifex project), ONTRAM XINI (files extracted to an ONTRAM XINI package), Translation Table (files extracted to tab-delimited files), and XLIFF 2.0 (files extracted to XLIFF v2 documents). Each package type may have its own specific options. Not all types of package have configurable options.
notes: The default writer class 'net.sf.okapi.steps.rainbowkit.xliff.XLIFFPackageWriter' corresponds to the Generic XLIFF package format.

packageName.description: The name of the package directory to create within the output directory. This becomes the directory name containing the translation kit files.

packageDirectory.description: The root of the output directory where the package will be created. You can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir}, as well as any of the source or target locale variables (${srcLoc}, ${trgLoc}, etc.).

supportFiles.description: A list of support material files to include in the package. Each item specifies an origin path and a destination path within the package. The origin path supports * and ? wildcard characters (e.g., C:\myProj\myExtra.* will copy all files starting with 'myExtra.' from that directory). The destination path must start with \ or / indicating the root of the package directory; use <same> to keep the original filename. If the origin does not use wildcards, the destination can specify a different name to rename the copied file. Both origin and destination support the variables ${rootDir}, ${inputRootDir}, and locale variables (${srcLoc}, ${trgLoc}, etc.).

message.description: An optional message to include with the translation package.

outputManifest.description: When true, outputs a manifest file with the translation package that contains metadata needed for merging the translated files back.

createZip.description: When true, creates a ZIP file from the generated package. The ZIP file has the same name as the project and is placed in the same directory with a .rkp extension.

sendOutput.description: When true, sends the prepared files to the next step as raw documents instead of the filter events of the original input file. This allows further processing of the extracted translation kit files in subsequent pipeline steps.
notes: This option has no effect if the selected package format does not support one output file for each input file.

limitations: If this step is used in a pipeline that modifies the input file (e.g. with a Search and Replace Step), the 'original' files saved in the generated project may not be usable to merge back the translated files. For example, a search and replace may result in more or less inline codes. You would need to change those files so they reflect the content that was really used at the point the content was extracted.
processingNotes: The step supports locale variables (${srcLoc}, ${trgLoc}, etc.) in directory paths, package names, and support file paths.
The step can be combined with segmentation and leveraging steps in a pipeline to segment text and pre-translate before creating the kit.
Package formats that do not support one output file per input file will ignore the sendOutput option.

examples.title: Simple extraction pipeline
examples.description: The simplest pipeline to create a translation kit: convert raw documents to filter events, then create the kit.
examples.input: Raw Document to Filter Events Step → Rainbow Translation Kit Creation Step

examples.title: Pre-translated extraction pipeline
examples.description: A more advanced pipeline that segments text and applies leveraging from multiple translation resources before creating the kit.
examples.input: Raw Document to Filter Events Step → Segmentation Step → First Leveraging Step → Second Leveraging Step → Rainbow Translation Kit Creation Step

filter-events-to-raw-document.filterName: Filter Events to Raw Document Step
filter-events-to-raw-document.overview: This step merges back extracted content coming as filter events into its original format. It takes filter events as input and sends a raw document as output. To create the initial filter events from an input document, use the Raw Document to Filter Events Step. A simple extract-then-merge pipeline can be constructed by chaining Raw Document to Filter Events Step followed by this step.
limitations: None known.
processingNotes: This step has no user-configurable parameters.
Expects filter events as input and produces a raw document as output, or NO_OP if writing to a stream.
Uses pipeline-level parameter mappings for OUTPUT_ENCODING, OUTPUT_URI, SOURCE_LOCALE, and TARGET_LOCALE rather than step-specific parameters.

examples.title: Simple extract-merge pipeline
examples.description: A minimal pipeline that extracts a document into filter events and then merges them back into the original format. This is the simplest way to round-trip a document through Okapi filters.
examples.input:
Pipeline:
1. Raw Document to Filter Events Step
2. Filter Events to Raw Document Step
examples.output: The original document reconstructed from filter events.

format-conversion.overview: This step creates output in a given format using the data extracted from an input document, allowing conversion from one format to another. It takes filter events and sends filter events. The documents generated by this step are not meant to be merged back into their original format; for round-trip purposes, use the Raw Document to Filter Events Step and Filter Events to Raw Document Step instead.

singleOutput.description: When set, generates one output document for all input documents. If not set, one output document is created for each input document.
notes: When enabled, the 'outputPath' field becomes active for specifying the single output file path.
When enabled, the 'autoExtensions' option is not available.

autoExtensions.description: When set, output files are created with paths that are the same as the input file with an additional extension denoting the format of the output (e.g., .po, .tmx, etc.). If not set, the outputs will be the ones specified by the calling application.

dependsOn.condition: must be false (i.e., 'Create a single output document' must not be set)

targetStyle.description: Specifies the type of content to put in the target field of the output. Integer value selecting between different target content strategies.

outputPath.description: The full path of the single output document to generate. For a Pensieve TM, the output must be a directory. The variable ${rootDir} can be used in the path.

dependsOn.condition: must be true (field is enabled only when 'Create a single output document' is set)

notes: For Pensieve TM output, this must be a directory path, not a file path.
Supports the ${rootDir} variable in the path.

outputFormat.description: Selects the type of output file to generate. Available formats: 'po' (PO File — Gettext PO format, encoded in UTF-8), 'tmx' (TMX Document — Translation Memory eXchange format), 'table' (Tab-Delimited Table — tab-delimited text in UTF-8 with source in first column and target in second, literal tabs escaped as \t), 'pensieve' (Pensieve TM database), 'corpus' (Parallel Corpus Files — two plain text files with inline codes stripped, one entry per line, locale code appended to filenames, used for systems like Moses MT), 'wordtable' (Word Table — RTF table format with generic inline codes, one entry per row; for TTX input, match percentage is shown in the Type column).

useGenericCodes.description: When set, uses generic inline codes in the output document. A generic inline code takes the form <N>, </N>, and <N/> where N is the number identifier of the code. If not set, inline codes are output in their original format (except for references). If the output format is TMX, this option is ignored and the inline codes are always in TMX format.
notes: This option is ignored when the output format is TMX; TMX always uses its own inline code format.

skipEntriesWithoutText.description: When set, entries in the input document that have no text content (i.e., they are empty or contain only inline codes) are not included in the output.

approvedEntriesOnly.description: When set, only entries that have the target property 'approved' set to 'yes' are included in the output. For example, 'fuzzy' entries in a PO file have this property set to 'no'. Entries without the approved property are assumed to be non-approved and will be excluded.
notes: Entries without the approved property are assumed to be non-approved.

overwriteSameSource.description: Applicable to Pensieve TM output only. When set, if an entry to import has the same source text as one or more entries in the existing TM, all matching existing entries will be replaced by the new one. Do not set this option if you want to allow different translations for the same source.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to Pensieve TM format

notes: Only applicable when the output format is Pensieve TM.
When enabled, ALL matching existing entries with the same source text are replaced by the new entry.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: PO and Tab-Delimited Table outputs are encoded in UTF-8.
Tab-Delimited Table format escapes literal tab characters as \t; first column is source, second column is target.
