search-and-replace.overview: This step performs search and replace actions on either the text units or the full content of input documents. It can take as input either a raw document (operating on the whole file) or filter events (operating on text unit content). Patterns are processed in the order they are declared, and both plain text and Java regular expression modes are supported. The step sends back the same type as its input: updated filter events or an updated raw document.

regEx.description: Enables regular expression mode for all search and replace patterns. When enabled, search patterns are interpreted as Java regular expressions (java.util.regex.Pattern), and replacement strings support group references ($1, $2, etc.) where $1 corresponds to the first captured group of the search expression. When disabled, patterns support only literal text with escape sequences (\n, \r, \t, \\, \uHHHH). Enabling this option also makes the dotAll, multiLine, and ignoreCase options accessible.
notes: The placeholders for search group references in replacement strings are "$1", "$2", etc. See Matcher.replaceAll() documentation for full details.
In all cases (regex on or off), you must use \\ to represent a literal backslash. For any sequence \C where C is not a special case, the result is the literal character itself.

dotAll.description: Changes the meaning of the period character '.' so that it matches every character instead of every character except \n (line-feed). This is the DOTALL flag in Java regex. Applies to all expressions, not just the currently selected one.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

ignoreCase.description: Ignores case differences when matching patterns. For example, 'bear' matches 'Bearcat', 'BEARCAT', and 'bearcat'. This is the CASE_INSENSITIVE flag in Java regex. Applies to all expressions, not just the currently selected one.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

multiLine.description: Changes the meaning of '^' and '$' so that they match at the beginning and end of any line, not just the beginning and end of the whole string. This is the MULTILINE flag in Java regex. Applies to all expressions, not just the currently selected one.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

target.description: When processing text units (filter events input), performs the search and replace on the target content. Enabled by default. This option is ignored if the step is processing a raw document.
notes: When working on the target content, you may have to use the Create Target Step before this step to make sure there is content in the target.

source.description: When processing text units (filter events input), performs the search and replace on the source content. Not enabled by default. This option is ignored if the step is processing a raw document.

replaceALL.description: When enabled, replaces all matches found. When disabled, only the first match is replaced. When processing text units, this controls whether all or only one occurrence is replaced within each text unit. When processing the whole file (raw document), this controls whether all or only one occurrence is replaced for the entire file.

replacementsPath.description: Path to a tab-delimited, UTF-8 encoded, two-column text file containing additional replacements. The first column contains search strings and the second column contains replacement strings. This file is processed separately AFTER the table expressions have been processed. The replacements from this file are limited to literal searches only (non-regex, true substring matching). Supports ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} variables in the path.
notes: The replacements in the tab-delimited file are currently limited to literal searches (non-regex and true sub-strings).
This file is not loaded into the UI table but processed separately after the table expressions.

logPath.description: Path to the file where a log of performed replacements will be saved. Supports ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} variables in the path. Default value is ${rootDir}/replacementsLog.txt.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

saveLog.description: When enabled, saves a log of which replacements have been made to the file specified by logPath.

count.description: Tracks the number of search/replace items defined in the patterns list. Each item has three properties: whether it is enabled (use), the search pattern, and the replacement pattern. Items are processed in the order they appear in the list.
notes: Make sure that longer patterns are placed first in the list if there is a chance of overlapping matches.

limitations: When working with a raw document as input, the whole content of the document is loaded in memory. This may result in problems with very large documents.
The replacements from the external tab-delimited file (replacementsPath) are limited to literal searches only (non-regex, true substring matching).
processingNotes: If the step receives filter events, search and replace operates on text unit content and sends updated filter events. If it receives a raw document, it operates on the whole file content and sends an updated raw document.
When processing a raw document, the file must be in a text-based format — the document is seen exactly as it would be in a text editor with no conversion of escaped characters.
Patterns are processed in the order they are declared in the list. Longer patterns should be placed first if there is a chance of overlapping matches.
The external replacements file (replacementsPath) is processed AFTER the table expressions.

Both plain text and regex modes support the escape sequences: \n (line-feed), \r (carriage-return), \t (tab), \\ (backslash), \uHHHH (Unicode character). Regex replace additionally supports \$ (literal dollar sign) and $N (group reference).
The source and target options are ignored when processing a raw document.

examples.title: Plain text search and replace
examples.description: When regular expressions are OFF, patterns support literal text with escape sequences. Search for a literal string and replace with another. Special sequences \n, \r, \t, \\, and \uHHHH are supported in both search and replace fields.
examples.output: All occurrences of 'old-term' are replaced with 'new-term' in the target content of each text unit.

examples.title: Regex search with group references
examples.description: When regular expressions are ON, search patterns use Java regex syntax and replacements can reference captured groups using $1, $2, etc. The dotAll, multiLine, and ignoreCase flags apply globally to all patterns.
examples.output: Email-like patterns are reformatted, e.g., 'user@example.com' becomes '[user at example.com]'.

examples.title: External replacements file
examples.description: A tab-delimited UTF-8 file can provide additional literal replacements processed after the main pattern list. Each line has two columns: search string (tab) replacement string.
examples.output: After all table patterns are applied, literal substring replacements from the file are applied in order.

segmentation.overview: The Segmentation step segments the content of extracted text units into smaller parts, usually corresponding to sentences, using SRX 2.0 (Segmentation Rules eXchange) rules. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. Text units flagged as non-translatable, text units with no content, and text units that are already segmented (unless overwrite is enabled) are not segmented.

segmentSource.description: When enabled, segments the source text of the text units using the specified SRX rules document. The SRX document path can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir}.
notes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are not segmented.
Text units with no content are not segmented.

segmentTarget.description: When enabled, segments the existing target text of the text units using the specified SRX rules document, if there is text for the target locales being processed. Only the locales currently being processed are affected, even if the document contains more locales. The SRX document can be the same as for source segmentation. The path can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir}.
notes: Only target locales being processed are affected, even if the document contains additional locales.

renumberCodes.description: When enabled, changes the IDs of inline codes in each resulting segment so they all start at '1'. This can be useful when working with translation memories where each segment restarts the IDs of its inline codes. The 'Restore original IDs to renumbered codes' option in the Desegmentation Step can be used to restore the original IDs when desegmenting.
notes: Warning: This option cannot work with documents where the ID values are not numeric, or not sequential.
Using this option when creating a translation kit requires a separate Desegmentation Step when merging back, and may not be possible with some formats that are segment-based like TTX.

checkSegments.description: When enabled, verifies that if there is a target available, all source segments have a corresponding target segment in the target content. This also verifies that both source and target have the same number of segments.
notes: This verification does not ensure that the content of a target segment is the translation of its corresponding source text. It only matches their segment ID.

trimSrcLeadingWS.description: Controls trimming of leading whitespace from source segments. A value of -1 means no trimming (default behavior).

trimSrcTrailingWS.description: Controls trimming of trailing whitespace from source segments. A value of -1 means no trimming (default behavior).

trimTrgLeadingWS.description: Controls trimming of leading whitespace from target segments. A value of -1 means no trimming (default behavior).

trimTrgTrailingWS.description: Controls trimming of trailing whitespace from target segments. A value of -1 means no trimming (default behavior).

forceSegmentedOutput.description: When enabled, forces file formats that can represent segmentation to show the segments, regardless of what is set in the filter configuration. For example, the XLIFF Filter by default outputs segments only for entries that had segments in the input XLIFF document. When this option is set, the filter's output option is changed to show segments as needed, even for text units that had no segments in the input. If this option is off, segments are shown only if the filter configuration allows it.

overwriteSegmentation.description: Controls behavior when input text is already segmented. When enabled, if a text unit is already segmented, it is re-segmented against the SRX rules specified in the step parameters. Previous segmentation will be lost. When disabled (and deepenSegmentation is also disabled), existing segmentation is preserved unchanged.
notes: This is one of three mutually exclusive segmentation behaviors: keep existing (both overwrite and deepen off), overwrite (this option on), or deepen (deepenSegmentation on).

deepenSegmentation.description: Controls behavior when input text is already segmented. When enabled, keeps existing segmentation but tries to segment existing segments further, creating more segments and text parts out of existing segments. Segmentation gets deeper only if the new SRX rules in the step parameters provide higher granularity than the original segmentation.
notes: This is one of three mutually exclusive segmentation behaviors: keep existing (both overwrite and deepen off), overwrite (overwriteSegmentation on), or deepen (this option on).

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: When enabled, treats isolated inline codes as whitespace during segmentation. This can affect where segment breaks are placed when inline codes appear between potential segments.

doNotSegmentIfHasTarget.description: When enabled, skips segmentation of a text unit if it already has target content. This can be used to preserve pre-existing aligned source-target pairs without applying segmentation.

limitations: Strict implementation of SRX syntax in Java is not possible. See the 'SRX and Java' page for details on the limitations.
Using the 'Renumber code IDs' option when creating a translation kit requires a separate Desegmentation Step when merging back, and may not be possible with some segment-based formats like TTX.
The renumber codes option cannot work with documents where inline code ID values are not numeric or not sequential.
processingNotes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are not segmented.
Text units with no content are not segmented.
Text units that are already segmented are not re-segmented unless the 'overwriteSegmentation' or 'deepenSegmentation' option is set.
SRX document paths support ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} variable substitution.
The step uses SRX 2.0 rules, which define break and non-break positions using regular expression patterns.

The separation between text units is based on the structure of the original file format (e.g., each

element in HTML produces a separate text unit); this step further breaks down text unit content into segments.

sentence-aligner.overview: This step aligns the sentences of each text unit from two documents. It takes filter events and sends filter events. The aligner algorithm finds the best possible alignment based on character lengths of sentences, with internal parameters accounting for language-specific character length ratios. Possible match types produced include 1-1, 2-1, 1-2, 0-1, 1-0, 2-0, 2-3, 3-2, etc. The step can optionally output results as a TMX file or as bilingual text units.

generateTMX.description: When set to true, generates a TMX file with the aligned entries and the step returns the events it received (possibly re-segmented). When set to false, the step returns the bilingual text units corresponding to the alignment (also segmented). If the TMX output file already exists it will be overwritten.
notes: Currently the events sent by this step when generateTMX is true are the same as the events it received (and not the generated aligned text units). Bilingual text unit output will be implemented in a future version.

tmxOutputPath.description: Full path of the output TMX file to generate. If the file already exists it will be overwritten.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for the TMX file to be generated

segmentSource.description: Set this option to segment the source content before trying to align it. If this option is not set, the content is expected to be already segmented. If this option is set and the content is already segmented, the existing segmentation will be reset to the new one.

useCustomSourceRules.description: Set this option to use a specified SRX file for segmenting the source. If this option is not set and segmentation is required, the default rules are used.
notes: The default rules are hard-coded with the step. They are not the rules defined in the config sub-directory of the installation directory.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for segmentation rules to take effect

customSourceRulesPath.description: Full path of the SRX file to use for segmenting the source.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this path to be used

segmentTarget.description: Set this option to segment the target content before trying to align it. If this option is not set, the content is expected to be already segmented. If this option is set and the content is already segmented, the existing segmentation will be reset to the new one.

useCustomTargetRules.description: Set this option to use a specified SRX file for segmenting the target. If this option is not set and segmentation is required, the default rules are used.
notes: The default rules are hard-coded with the step. They are not the rules defined in the config sub-directory of the installation directory.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for segmentation rules to take effect

customTargetRulesPath.description: Full path of the SRX file to use for segmenting the target.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this path to be used

collapseWhitespace.description: Collapse whitespace (space, newline, etc.) to a single space before performing the segmentation and alignment.

outputOneTOneMatchesOnly.description: Set this option to output only 1-1 sentence aligned matches, filtering out all other match types (2-1, 1-2, 0-1, 1-0, etc.).
notes: This option is overridden by forceSimpleOneToOneAlignment when that option is enabled.

forceSimpleOneToOneAlignment.description: For each paragraph, if there are the same number of sentences, align the sentences one-to-one. Otherwise, join the sentences in the paragraph back together and output the aligned paragraph. When enabled, this overrides the outputOneTOneMatchesOnly option.
notes: If checked, this overrides the Output 1-1 matches only option.

processingNotes: Text units from the source and target documents must be perfectly synchronized (aligned). If the source document has more text units than the target document, an error will be generated.
The aligner algorithm finds the best possible alignment based on character lengths of sentences, with internal parameters that account for language-specific expansion/contraction ratios.
Entries set as non-translatable are not processed.
When processing bilingual documents (e.g., TMX, PO), use only a single input list.
Currently the events sent by this step are the same as the events it received (not the generated aligned text units) when generating TMX. This will be implemented in a future version.
limitations: None known.

simple-word-count.overview: This step performs word counts on the source content of the text units of a set of documents. It is intended for fast and simple word counts; for more sophisticated counts, see the Word Count Step. The word counts are saved as annotations that can be used in other steps, such as the Translation Comparison Step which can use the word count annotation in its report. Takes filter events and sends filter events.
limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Word counts are saved as annotations on text units that can be consumed by downstream steps (e.g., Translation Comparison Step).
This is a lightweight counting step; for more sophisticated counts use the Word Count Step.

space-check.overview: This step fixes spaces around target in-line codes within the text units of a given document. Spaces are either inserted or removed from the target segments so that the spacing around each in-line code in the source is matched. If there is a mismatched number of in-line codes between the source and the target, the un-matched in-line codes are skipped and the remainder of the text unit is processed. Takes filter events and sends filter events.
processingNotes: Spaces are inserted or removed from target segments to match the spacing around each in-line code in the source.
When there is a mismatched number of in-line codes between source and target, un-matched in-line codes are skipped and the remainder of the text unit is still processed.
limitations: None known.

t-m-import.overview: The TM Import step imports text units from an input document into a Pensieve TM. Each text unit is imported into the specified TM, and if the text unit is segmented, each segment is imported individually. The step takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output.

tmDirectory.description: Full path of the directory of the TM where to import the text units. If the TM does not exist, it is created automatically. If the TM already exists, the new entries are added to the existing ones. The variable `${rootDir}` can be used in the path to reference the root directory.
notes: Supports the ${rootDir} variable in the path
Creates the TM if it does not already exist
Adds entries to existing TM if it already exists

overwriteSameSource.description: When enabled, if an entry to import has the same source text as one or more entries in the existing TM, all matching existing entries will be replaced by the new one. When disabled, different translations for the same source text are allowed to coexist in the TM.
notes: When set, ALL matching existing entries with the same source text are replaced by the new one, not just the first match
Do not set this option if you want to allow different translations for the same source

processingNotes: If a text unit is segmented and the number of segments differs between the source and the target, the whole text unit is imported instead of individual segments.
If a text unit does not have text for the specified target locale, that text unit is skipped and not imported.
Each text unit is imported individually; segmented text units have each segment imported separately (unless segment counts differ between source and target).

t-t-x-joiner.overview: The TTX Joiner step re-joins multiple TTX documents that were previously split (by the TTX Splitter Step) back into a single document. It takes raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output. The step outputs the resulting file in the same directory as the input documents, sorting input files internally to ensure proper part ordering before concatenation.

suffix.description: A marker text to add at the end of the output filename. For example, if set to '_CONCAT', an input file 'index.html_part001.ttx' would produce 'index.html_CONCAT.ttx'. If a file with the same name already exists in the output directory, it will be overridden without warning.
notes: If a file with the same name already exists in the output directory, it will be overridden without warning.

limitations: This step is triggered at the END_BATCH event.
Input files with no part marker, or invalid part marker will be ignored (with a warning).
The received event is unaltered — the step only outputs the resulting joined file to the same directory as the input documents.
processingNotes: The input file list is sorted internally before processing so all parts of each original document are listed in the proper order.
The step outputs the resulting file in the same directory as the input documents.
Input files with no part marker or invalid part marker will be ignored with a warning.

examples.title: Joining split TTX files with a suffix
examples.description: Demonstrates how multiple split TTX part files are sorted and joined back into their original documents with a custom suffix appended to the filename.
examples.output:
Files are first sorted internally:
  C:\Project\FolderABC\index.html_part001.ttx
  C:\Project\FolderABC\index.html_part002.ttx
  C:\Project\FolderABC\someFile.html_part001.ttx
  C:\Project\FolderABC\someFile.html_part002.ttx

Output files:
  C:\Project\FolderABC\index.html_CONCAT.ttx
  C:\Project\FolderABC\someFile.html_CONCAT.ttx

t-t-x-splitter.overview: The TTX Splitter step splits a single TTX document into a given number of documents of roughly equal size, calculated based on the total source word count of the original input. It takes raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output. The new documents are output at the same location as the input document with an extra marker and number appended to the file name, which is later used by the TTX Joiner Step to reassemble the files.

partCount.description: The number of output files to create from one input document. The step will split the source TTX document into this many parts of roughly equal word count. Must be an integer; defaults to 2.
notes: The extra part appended to each output file name (marker + number) is used later by the TTX Joiner Step to reassemble the split documents.

limitations: Works on segmented TTX files only.
processingNotes: The received event is passed through unaltered; the step outputs the new split documents at the same location as the input document.
Output file names include an extra marker and number suffix that the TTX Joiner Step uses to reassemble the documents.
Document splitting is based on total source word count of the original input, aiming for roughly equal parts.

examples.title: Split TTX into 3 parts
examples.description: Splitting a single TTX document into 3 roughly equal parts by word count. The output files are created alongside the original input with marker suffixes for later rejoining via the TTX Joiner Step.

terminology-leveraging.overview: This step leverages glossary terms into extracted text units by sending segment lists to a terminology resource and retrieving matching glossary terms. It takes filter text unit events and sends back filter text unit events with terminology annotations. The terminology resource connector splits segments into source terms or applies other techniques to find existing glossary terms for each segment. Text units flagged as non-translatable are skipped.

leverage.description: Master switch to enable or disable the terminology leveraging step. When disabled, the step remains in the pipeline but does not process any text units. This allows you to keep the step configured in your pipeline and toggle it on and off without removing it.

annotateSource.description: When enabled, source unit segments are annotated with found glossary entries. Annotation is performed only if the term is found within the source segment text. This allows downstream steps or tools to identify which parts of the source text match terminology entries.

annotateTarget.description: When enabled, target unit segments are annotated with found glossary entries. Annotation is performed only if the translation of the term is found within the target segment text. This allows downstream steps or tools to identify which parts of the target text match terminology translations.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for annotations to be applied

connectorClassName.description: The fully qualified class name of the terminology resource connector to use. It can be a subclass of the BaseTerminologyConnector abstract class or a custom implementation of the ITerminologyQuery interface. The connector is responsible for querying the terminology resource and returning matching glossary entries for each segment.
notes: The terminology resource connector is capable of defining its own strategy for generation of glossary entry IDs and glossary translation IDs.

connectorParameters.description: Configuration parameters for the selected terminology resource connector. These settings are specific to the terminology resource selected via the connector class name and control how the connector communicates with or queries the underlying terminology resource.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to a valid connector class

limitations: It is not possible to chain several steps if you want to leverage against several terminology resources.
processingNotes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are skipped and will not be sent to the terminology resource.
The terminology resource connector defines its own strategy for generation of glossary entry IDs and glossary translation IDs.
The step processes filter events in-place: it takes filter text unit events and sends back filter text unit events with terminology annotations added.

text-modification.overview: The Text Modification step modifies the content of text units in a document pipeline. It can perform various character substitutions (replacing with Xs/Ns, Extended Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, or Chinese characters), add prefixes/suffixes, append metadata, mark segments, and expand text length. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. Text units set as non-translatable are not modified.

type.description: Selects the kind of change to apply to text content. Available options: 0 = Keep the original text; 1 = Replace letters with Xs and digits with Ns; 2 = Remove text but keep inline codes; 3 = Replace selected ASCII characters with Extended Latin characters; 4 = Replace selected ASCII characters with Cyrillic characters; 5 = Replace selected ASCII characters with Arabic characters; 6 = Replace selected ASCII characters with Chinese characters.
notes: The result of the character substitution is not meant to have any specific meaning beyond being a set of characters in a given script. This function does not perform a translation, transliteration, or any other meaningful linguistic operation.

addPrefix.description: When enabled, adds a prefix at the start of each text unit. The actual prefix text is specified by the 'prefix' parameter.

prefix.description: The text to prepend at the start of each text unit when addPrefix is enabled. Default value is '{START_'.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

addSuffix.description: When enabled, adds a suffix at the end of each text unit. The actual suffix text is specified by the 'suffix' parameter.

suffix.description: The text to append at the end of each text unit when addSuffix is enabled. Default value is '_END}'.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

applyToExistingTarget.description: When enabled, applies the changes also to text units that already have a translation (e.g. for multilingual files). By default, text units with existing translations are not modified.

addName.description: When enabled, appends the name of each text unit at the end of its value. If the text unit has no name associated, the extraction ID is added instead.

addID.description: When enabled, appends the extraction ID of each text unit at the end of its value. Extraction IDs are filter-specific.

markSegments.description: When enabled, adds '[' and ']' delimiters around segments in each text unit. The delimiters are placed just around the text — after the prefix if one is added, and before the item name, extraction ID, and suffix if they are added. If the text unit is not segmented, the delimiters are added at the front and back of the full content of the text unit.

applyToBlankEntries.description: When enabled, applies the changes also to text units that have no text (i.e. are empty, or contain only white spaces or codes). Enabled by default.

expand.description: When enabled, expands the text to simulate translation-induced text growth. If the content is less than 31 characters, it is expanded by 50% or at least one character. If the content is longer than 30 characters, it is expanded by 100%. Empty strings are not expanded.

script.description: Integer value controlling the script used for character replacement. Works in conjunction with the type parameter when a script-based replacement mode is selected.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Text units set as non-translatable are not modified.
Text units with existing translations are modified only if the applyToExistingTarget option is enabled.
Character substitution results have no specific linguistic meaning — this is not translation or transliteration.
Text expansion uses different rates based on content length: 50% (minimum 1 character) for content under 31 characters, 100% for content 31 characters or longer. Empty strings are not expanded.
Segment markers are placed after prefix and before suffix/name/ID additions.

tokenization.overview: The Tokenization step creates annotations containing different tokenized forms of text units. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. It recognizes a wide range of token types including words, numbers, punctuation, dates, times, currencies, email addresses, internet addresses, emoticons, ideograms, and more. The step can tokenize source content, target content, or both, and supports locale-based filtering to control which languages are tokenized.

tokenizeSource.description: When enabled, the step tokenizes the content of the source text. This controls whether source-language text units are processed for token extraction.

tokenizeTargets.description: When enabled, the step tokenizes the content of the different selected targets. This controls whether target-language text units are processed for token extraction.

WORD.description: A run of characters constituting a word of the given language. This is a locale-sensitive token type that relies on the language rules for the specified locale to identify word boundaries.

HYPHENATED_WORD.description: A word including hyphens of various types. Matches compound words joined by hyphens.

NUMBER.description: Numbers, including any commas or points symbols. Matches numeric values with optional thousands separators and decimal points.

PUNCTUATION.description: Punctuation characters as defined by the Unicode Consortium standards.

WHITESPACE.description: Whitespace characters as defined by the Unicode Consortium standards. Includes spaces, tabs, and other Unicode whitespace.

ABBREVIATION.description: Abbreviations like pct in 3.3pct, U.S., USD. Matches limited types of English abbreviations including percentage shorthand, dotted acronyms, and currency codes.

INTERNET.description: An Internet address (URI or IP). Matches URLs such as http://www.somesite.org/foo/index.html and IP addresses like 192.168.0.5.

EMAIL.description: E-mail addresses. Listed as 'E-MAIL' in the wiki token type table.

EMOTICON.description: Emoticon sequences like ":-)". Matches common text-based emoticon patterns.

EMOJI.description: All emoji characters defined in Unicode.
notes: Not listed in the wiki token type table; may be a newer addition.

CURRENCY.description: Sums in various currencies. The wiki specifically mentions US dollars, but the schema indicates broader currency support.
notes: The wiki describes this as 'Sums in US dollars', while the schema says 'Sums in various currencies'.

TIME.description: Time separated by either ":" or "." (24 hour, 12 hour with AM or PM). Matches time expressions in both common formats.

MARKUP.description: A run that begins with "<" and ends with ">" like in HTML and XML. Matches inline markup tags that may appear in text content.

OTHER_SYMBOL.description: Various Unicode symbols (mathematical etc.). Matches symbol characters from the Unicode Symbol categories.
notes: Not listed in the wiki token type table; may be a newer addition.

KANA.description: Hiragana, Katakana (Japanese). Matches runs of Japanese syllabary characters.

IDEOGRAM.description: Ideograms as defined by the Unicode Consortium standards. Matches CJK ideographic characters (Chinese, Japanese Kanji, Korean Hanja).

limitations: None known (per wiki).
processingNotes: The step supports locale filtering with a rich syntax: locale tags (e.g. 'en', 'en-us', 'en-us-win'), wildcards ('en-*', '*-us'), regular expressions prefixed with @ (e.g. '@e[ns]-.+'), exclusion with ! prefix (e.g. 'en !en-nz'), and regex flags with ^ prefix (e.g. '^8').
Leave the locale filter empty to select all locales for tokenization.
Leave the token type selection empty to generate all available token types.
The wiki lists additional token types COMPANY and STOPWORD that are not present in the schema, suggesting they may be handled differently or removed from the configurable parameters.
The step requires both SOURCE_LOCALE and TARGET_LOCALE parameter mappings from the pipeline.

examples.title: Locale filter: all English except New Zealand
examples.description: Shows how to use the locale filter syntax with exclusion to tokenize all English locales except en-nz.
examples.output: Tokenizes en-us, en-gb, en-au, etc. but skips en-nz.

examples.title: Locale filter: regex for English and Spanish
examples.description: Uses a regular expression prefixed with @ to match all English (en-*) and Spanish (es-*) locales.
examples.output: Matches locale tags starting with 'en-' or 'es-' followed by one or more characters.

examples.title: Locale filter: regex with flags
examples.description: Shows how to append regex flags using the ^ prefix. The decimal value 8 corresponds to specific Java regex flags.
examples.output: Same as above but with Java regex flags applied (8 = MULTILINE).

translation-comparison.overview: The Translation Comparison step compares translations between two or three documents. The first document contains the base translations, while the second (and optionally third) contain translations to compare against the baseline. For each text unit, the step calculates ED-Score (Edit Distance based on Levenshtein distance) and FM-Score (Fuzzy Match based on Sørensen-Dice's Coefficient with 3-grams), both ranging from 0 (extreme difference) to 100 (no difference). The step outputs HTML reports, optional TMX documents, and tab-delimited score files, and computes summary statistics including an Edit Effort Score.

generateHTML.description: When enabled, creates one HTML file for each input document listing comparison results (source text, translation text, and scores). The HTML file name is the first document's name plus an .html extension. When this option is selected, the step also generates a tab-delimited file with the scores of each entry, named as the first document plus a .txt extension.
notes: Enabling this option also generates a tab-delimited .txt file alongside the HTML output.

autoOpen.description: When enabled, automatically opens the first HTML result file when the comparison process is completed.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for HTML files to exist to open

genericCodes.description: When enabled, the HTML report uses a generic representation for inline codes (e.g. <1>...</1>). If not set, the output uses the original code whenever possible. Note that some formats may have long and complicated original codes.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for HTML report to be generated

generateTMX.description: When enabled, creates a single TMX document containing all compared translations and their scores for all input documents. The path supports variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir}, as well as source or target locale variables (${srcLoc}, ${trgLoc}, etc.).

tmxPath.description: Full path of the TMX document to generate. Supports the variables ${rootDir}, ${inputRootDir}, and locale variables such as ${srcLoc} and ${trgLoc}.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for TMX output to be generated

target2Suffix.description: Suffix appended to the target language code for the second set of target entries in the TMX output. For example, enter '-mt' to indicate that the second target language is machine-translation generated text. If no suffix is entered, the language code for both target entries will be the same and the TMX document will include only the last one.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for TMX output to be generated

target3Suffix.description: Suffix appended to the target language code for the third set of target entries in the TMX output. Functions the same as the document 2 suffix but applies to the optional third document.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for TMX output to be generated

useAltTrans.description: When enabled and document 1 is an XLIFF file, uses the target of a given <alt-trans> element as the base target instead of the main target. This allows comparing, for example, a human translation done by a linguist with an MT candidate provided in the same XLIFF file — you specify the same file for both document 1 and 2.

altTransOrigin.description: The value of the 'origin' attribute of the <alt-trans> element to use as the base target. If no matching <alt-trans> element is found for a given trans-unit, that trans-unit will not be included in the report.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for alt-trans lookup to occur

notes: The wiki refers to this as the 'original' attribute but the schema names it 'altTransOrigin' with default 'BING', corresponding to the XLIFF alt-trans origin attribute.

document1Label.description: Optional label used in the report to identify the base translation (document 1).

document2Label.description: Optional label used in the report to identify the second translation (document 2).

document3Label.description: Optional label used in the report to identify the optional third translation (document 3).

caseSensitive.description: When enabled, case differences are taken into account when calculating the ED-Score and FM-Score. Disabling this option makes the comparison case-insensitive.

whitespaceSensitive.description: When enabled, whitespace differences are taken into account when calculating the ED-Score and FM-Score. Disabling this option ignores whitespace variations between compared texts.

punctuationSensitive.description: When enabled, punctuation differences are taken into account when calculating the ED-Score and FM-Score. Disabling this option ignores punctuation variations between compared texts.

useDataLog.description: When enabled, appends the average scores for each document at the end of a log file. The log file is a tab-delimited file in UTF-8 with fields: time stamp in UTC, path of the base document, path of the second document, edit-distance score, fuzzy score.

dataLogPath.description: Full path of the log file where average scores are appended. The log file is tab-delimited UTF-8 with fields: timestamp (UTC), base document path, second document path, edit-distance score, and fuzzy score.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for logging to occur

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Input documents must have the same number of text units and the text units must be in the same order (but different events between text units are allowed).
Two scoring metrics are calculated: ED-Score (Edit Distance based on Levenshtein distance) and FM-Score (Fuzzy Match based on Sørensen-Dice's Coefficient with 3-grams), both ranging from 0 to 100.
Neither score is very accurate on short segments.

The HTML report includes average scores. If text units have word count annotations (e.g., from the Simple Word Count Step or Word Count Step placed before this step), the report will include word counts for each source segment.
The Edit Effort Score is calculated as: 100 - (Average ED-Score by word + Average FM-Score by word) / 2. A score of 0 means no change; 100 means the translation was entirely rewritten.
The summary section includes: Total Number of Segments, Total Number of Words, Average word count per segment, Average ED/FM-Score (by segment and by word), and Edit Effort Score.

examples.title: Data log output format
examples.description: Example of the tab-delimited log file entry produced when 'Append the average results to a log' is enabled, showing timestamp, document paths, and scores.
examples.output: 2013-04-01 16:09:14+0000	/C:/myProject1/machine.tmx	/C:/myProject1/human.tmx	93.75	96.50

examples.title: Comparing MT output with human translation using alt-trans
examples.description: Use the same XLIFF file for both document 1 and 2, with useAltTrans enabled, to compare a human translation with an MT candidate provided as an alt-trans element in the XLIFF file.
examples.input: useAltTrans: true, altTransOrigin: "BING", document1: same-file.xlf, document2: same-file.xlf
examples.output: HTML report showing per-segment ED-Score and FM-Score comparing the main target (human translation) against the alt-trans target (MT candidate).

uri-conversion.overview: This step converts the translatable content of an input document to and from URI escape notation. It can either un-escape URI escape sequences into normal text, or escape normal text characters into URI escape sequences. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output.

conversionType.description: Controls the direction of URI conversion. A value of 0 selects 'Un-escape the URI escape sequences', which converts URI escaped sequences into normal text. A value of 1 selects 'Escape content to URI escape sequences', which converts normal text into text where characters are escaped into URI escape sequences. Which characters get escaped when using escape mode is determined by the escapeList parameter.

updateAll.description: When enabled, converts all non-ASCII (extended) characters to URI escape sequences. This applies when escaping content to URI escape sequences.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to escape mode (1)

escapeList.description: A string containing the list of characters to escape when converting to URI escape sequences. Each character in the string will be escaped. Note that the '%' character is always escaped regardless of whether it appears in this list. The UI provides two preset buttons: 'All But Marks' resets the list to all characters except those specified as 'marks' in RFC 2396 (which are: '-', '_', '.', '!', '~', '*', ''', '(', and ')'). 'All But Marks And Reserved' resets the list to all characters except both the RFC 2396 'marks' and the 'reserved' characters (which are: ';', '/', '?', ':', '@', '&', '=', '+', '$', ',').
notes: The '%' character is always escaped regardless of this list.

dependsOn.condition: must be set to escape mode (1)

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: When un-escaping, URI escape sequences (e.g., %20) are converted back to their original characters.
When escaping, the step uses UTF-8 encoding for converting non-ASCII characters to URI percent-encoded sequences as per RFC 2396.

whitespace-correction.overview: This step simplifies the addition or removal of inter-segment whitespace when translating to or from Chinese or Japanese scripts that do not typically use spaces. When translating from a space-delimited language to a non-space-delimited language, whitespace following segment-ending punctuation is removed; in the reverse direction, whitespace is added. The step also converts between full-width and standard punctuation forms (e.g., Ideographic Full Stop U+3002 to/from period). It performs no action when translating between two space-delimited languages or between Chinese and Japanese.

punctuation.description: Comma-separated list of punctuation classes to which the whitespace correction should be applied. Each class controls both whitespace adjustment and punctuation form conversion between full-width/ideographic and standard forms. Valid values are: FULL_STOP (converts Ideographic Full Stop U+3002 and Full-width Full Stop U+FF0E to/from a period), COMMA (converts Ideographic Comma U+3001 and Full-width Comma U+FF0C to/from a comma), EXCLAMATION_MARK (converts Full-width Exclamation Mark U+FF01 to/from an exclamation point), QUESTION_MARK (converts Full-width Question Mark U+FF1F to/from a question mark). By default all four punctuation classes are enabled.

whitespace.description: Comma-separated list of Unicode whitespace character names that the step considers as whitespace for removal or addition operations. The default value includes a comprehensive set of Unicode whitespace characters: standard spaces (SPACE, NO_BREAK_SPACE), line breaks (LINE_FEED, CARRIAGE_RETURN, NEXT_LINE, LINE_SEPARATOR, PARAGRAPH_SEPARATOR), tabs (CHARACTER_TABULATION, LINE_TABULATION), form feeds (FORM_FEED), typographic spaces (EN_QUAD, EM_QUAD, EN_SPACE, EM_SPACE, THREE_PER_EM_SPACE, FOUR_PER_EM_SPACE, SIX_PER_EM_SPACE, FIGURE_SPACE, PUNCUATION_SPACE, THIS_SPACE, HAIR_SPACE, NARROW_NO_BREAK_SPACE, MEDIUM_MATHEMATICAL_SPACE), ideographic space (IDEOGRAPHIC_SPACE), and zero-width characters (ZERO_WIDTH_SPACE, ZERO_WIDTH_NON_BREAKING_SPACE).

limitations: Relies on the assumption that each source segment contains a single sentence and has been translated to a single sentence in the target language, so results may be incorrect for multi-sentence segments.
Performs no action when translating between two space-delimited languages (e.g., English to French) or between Chinese and Japanese.
processingNotes: The step operates on filter events (takes filter events, sends filter events) and processes text units.
The step requires both SOURCE_LOCALE and TARGET_LOCALE to determine whether to add or remove whitespace based on whether each locale uses space-delimited script.
When translating from a space-delimited to a non-space-delimited language, whitespace following segment-ending punctuation is removed.
When translating from a non-space-delimited to a space-delimited language, whitespace is added following segment-ending punctuation.
In addition to whitespace adjustment, the step converts punctuation between full-width/ideographic forms and standard ASCII forms as appropriate for the target locale.

examples.title: English to Chinese translation whitespace removal
examples.description: When translating from English (space-delimited) to Chinese (non-space-delimited), the step removes whitespace after segment-ending punctuation and converts standard punctuation to full-width/ideographic equivalents. For example, a period followed by a space would become an Ideographic Full Stop (U+3002) with no trailing space.

examples.title: Chinese to English translation whitespace addition
examples.description: When translating from Chinese (non-space-delimited) to English (space-delimited), the step adds whitespace after segment-ending punctuation and converts full-width/ideographic punctuation to standard ASCII equivalents. For example, an Ideographic Full Stop (U+3002) would become a period followed by a space.

word-count.overview: This step performs word counts on the different parts of a set of documents. It takes filter events and sends filter events. The word counts are saved as annotations that can be used in other steps, such as the Scoping Report Step which can generate a report from the word counts. The word count generated follows the GMX-V 2.0 standard.

countInBatch.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the word count per batch. A word count annotation is always set to each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional annotation at the batch level.

countInBatchItems.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the word count per batch item. A word count annotation is always set to each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional annotation at the batch item level.

countInDocuments.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the word count per document. A word count annotation is always set to each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional annotation at the document level.

countInSubDocuments.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the word count per sub-document. A word count annotation is always set to each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional annotation at the sub-document level.

countInGroups.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the word count per group. A word count annotation is always set to each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional annotation at the group level.

bufferSize.description: Size of the text buffer used during word counting. A value of 0 indicates no buffering limit.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Word count annotations are always set on each text unit, regardless of the batch/document/group-level options.
The word count follows the GMX-V 2.0 standard (http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html).
Word count annotations can be consumed by downstream steps such as the Scoping Report Step.

x-m-l-analysis.overview: This step generates a report on the elements present in a set of input XML documents. It takes raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output. The report can be used to guess localization-related aspects of the input documents, such as which elements should have their content extracted, which elements are likely to be inline, and whether there are any CDATA sections.

outputPath.description: Full path of the result file where the analysis report will be written. The report contains information about the elements present in the input XML documents, including localization-related hints such as which elements should be extracted, which are likely inline, and whether CDATA sections exist.

autoOpen.description: When enabled, the result file is automatically opened after the analysis process is complete. Defaults to true.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The step processes a set of XML documents and produces a single analysis report.
Input and output type is raw document — the step passes through the raw documents unchanged while generating the report as a side effect.

x-m-l-char-fixing.overview: This step replaces characters that are invalid in XML by a marker. It takes a raw document as input and sends back a raw document. The step recognizes raw characters as well as numeric character references (NCRs), such as &#000B; or . The output is done in the same encoding as the input document, and Byte-Order-Marks are preserved if present.

replacement.description: The string used to replace invalid XML characters. Must be a valid Java Formatter string (java.util.Formatter). The parameter passed is always the integer value of the Unicode code-point. For example, to replace the invalid character by its hexadecimal representation placed between braces use: '{%X}'. If you want to remove the invalid characters, leave the replacement string empty. Note that some characters like '%' have special function in the Java Formatter syntax and may need to be escaped (e.g., '%%' to produce a literal '%').
notes: The replacement string must conform to the Java Formatter specification (java.util.Formatter).
The parameter passed to the formatter is always the integer value of the Unicode code-point.
To remove invalid characters entirely, leave the replacement string empty.
Some characters like '%' have special function in the Java Formatter syntax and may need to be escaped.

processingNotes: You need to specify the input encoding of the document. The only case where encoding specification is not required is for files with a Byte-Order-Mark.
The output is done in the same encoding as the input document.
Byte-Order-Marks are preserved if they are present in the input.
The step recognizes both raw characters and numeric character references (NCRs), such as &#000B; or .

examples.title: Hexadecimal representation with prefix
examples.description: Replace invalid character U+000B with a 3-digit zero-padded hexadecimal representation prefixed with 'x'.
examples.output: x0B

examples.title: NCR-like marker (default)
examples.description: Replace invalid character U+000B with an NCR-like marker using underscore prefix instead of ampersand.
examples.output: _#xB;

examples.title: Simple character replacement
examples.description: Replace invalid character U+000B with a question mark.

examples.title: Unicode replacement character NCR
examples.description: Replace invalid character U+000B with the Unicode replacement character U+FFFD as an NCR.
examples.output: �

examples.title: Decimal value with escaped percent signs
examples.description: Replace invalid character U+000B with its decimal code-point value surrounded by literal percent signs. The '%' characters must be escaped as '%%' in the Java Formatter syntax.

examples.title: Remove invalid characters
examples.description: To remove invalid characters entirely, leave the replacement string empty.
examples.output: (characters removed)

x-m-l-validation.overview: This step performs validation of XML input documents. It always checks for well-formedness, and can optionally perform DTD, W3C Schema, or RelaxNG Schema validation. It takes raw XML documents as input and sends raw XML documents as output, making it suitable for inserting into a pipeline to verify document integrity before further processing.

schemaPath.description: Full path of the XML Schema to use for validation. If performing a schema validation (W3C or RelaxNG), enter the path of the schema here. Any schema declared in the documents themselves will also be used in addition to the one specified here. For W3C and RelaxNG schemas, the path can be a URL. This field is only relevant when schema validation is selected.
notes: Any schema declared in the documents themselves will be used as well.
For W3C and RelaxNG schema the path can be a URL.

dependsOn.condition: must be true
must be set to W3C Schema or RelaxNG Schema validation

validate.description: Set this option to perform a DTD, W3C Schema, or RelaxNG Schema validation of the input documents in addition to the well-formedness verification. Well-formedness checking is always performed regardless of this setting.

validationType.description: Select the type of validation to perform. The available types are: DTD, W3C Schema, or RelaxNG Schema. When performing DTD validation, the DTD must be specified in the input documents.
notes: When performing a DTD validation, the DTD must be specified in the input documents.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

useFoundDTD.description: Set this option to validate the document against the DTDs found in the documents. If the option is not set, DTD declarations in the input documents are ignored.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Well-formedness checking is always performed regardless of whether structural validation is enabled.
When performing DTD validation, the DTD must be specified in the input documents themselves.

x-s-l-transform.overview: This step applies an XSLT template to input XML documents. It takes raw well-formed XML documents as input, applies the specified XSLT template, and produces a raw document output for the next step in the pipeline. The step supports custom XSLT parameters with variable substitution, and allows configuring custom transformer and XPath factory classes.

xsltPath.description: The full path of the XSLT template to apply on the input documents. Must point to a valid XSLT template file on the filesystem.

paramList.description: A list of parameters to pass to the XSLT template. The format must be name=value with one parameter per line (e.g., 'Lang=en\ntmFile=myTM.tmx'). You can use the following variables in parameter values: ${inputPath} / ${inputPath1} (full path of the first input document), ${inputURI} / ${inputURI1} (URI of the first input document), ${outputPath} / ${outputPath1} (full path of the first output document), ${srcLang} (language part of the source locale, e.g. 'de' for 'de-ch'), ${trgLang} (language part of the target locale, e.g. 'ja' for 'ja-jp'), ${inputPath2} / ${inputURI2} (full path/URI of the second input document), ${inputPath3} / ${inputURI3} (full path/URI of the third input document).

useCustomTransformer.description: When enabled, uses a custom XSLT transformer implementation and optionally a custom XPath engine, instead of the default ones provided by the current Java settings. The specified factory classes must be available on the Java classpath. The default transformer and XPath classes depend on the implementation and version of your Java Virtual Machine (see TransformerFactory.newInstance() documentation for lookup details).

factoryClass.description: The fully-qualified class name of the custom XSLT TransformerFactory implementation to use. For example: 'net.sf.saxon.TransformerFactoryImpl'. The class must be available on your Java classpath.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

xpathClass.description: The fully-qualified class name of the custom XPath factory implementation to use. For example: 'net.sf.saxon.xpath.XPathFactoryImpl'. This is optional even when useCustomTransformer is enabled. The class must be available on your Java classpath.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

passOnOutput.description: When true, the output of the XSLT transformation is passed as the raw document to the next step in the pipeline.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Input documents must be well-formed XML documents.

The default XSLT transformer and XPath classes depend on the implementation and version of the Java Virtual Machine. See the TransformerFactory.newInstance() documentation for details on how the class lookup is performed.
Variable substitution in parameters supports references to up to three input documents and one output document, as well as source and target language codes (language part only, not full locale).

examples.title: XSLT parameters with variables
examples.description: Passing parameters to an XSLT template using the name=value format, one per line. Demonstrates use of the ${inputPath2} variable to reference the second input document.

examples.title: Custom Saxon transformer configuration
examples.description: Configuring the step to use Saxon as the XSLT transformer and XPath engine instead of the JVM default. Requires useCustomTransformer to be enabled and the Saxon classes to be on the classpath.
examples.input:
useCustomTransformer: true
factoryClass: net.sf.saxon.TransformerFactoryImpl
xpathClass: net.sf.saxon.xpath.XPathFactoryImpl

xliff-joiner.overview: The XLIFF Joiner step re-joins multiple XLIFF documents into one or more documents based on a naming pattern defined by the input file marker parameter. The first document of each naming pattern group provides content up to the first <file> element and after the last <file> element, while subsequent documents contribute their <file> elements and any content between multiple <file> elements. It takes raw documents as input and sends raw documents as output. To perform the reverse operation, use the XLIFF Splitter Step.

inputFileMarker.description: The marker text used to identify and group files with similar naming patterns. Files whose names contain this marker followed by an index number (e.g., '_PART001', '_PART002') are grouped together and joined into a single output file. Files whose filenames do not contain the expected marker are skipped entirely.
notes: Files are joined in the order they arrive in the pipeline, regardless of the index number added by the XLIFF Splitter Step, due to sequential processing of files in the pipeline.

Subsequent documents of the same naming pattern group ignore content before their first <file> element and after their last <file> element, because the XLIFF Splitter Step writes out this content for each document.

outputFileMarker.description: The marker text used to generate the filename(s) of the output file(s). The input file marker portion of the filename is replaced with this output file marker to produce the output filename. For example, if the input file marker is '_PART' and the output file marker is '_CONCAT', then 'File1_PART001.xlf' becomes 'File1_CONCAT.xlf'.
notes: If files with the same names already exist in the output directory, they will be overridden without warning.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Because of the sequential processing of files in the pipeline, files are joined in the order they arrive in the pipeline, regardless of the index number added by the XLIFF Splitter Step.

Subsequent documents of the same naming pattern group ignore content before their first <file> element and after their last <file> element, since the XLIFF Splitter Step writes out this content for each document.
The received event is unaltered. The step outputs one or more output XLIFF documents depending on the input filenames pattern.
Files whose filenames do not contain the expected input file marker are skipped.

examples.title: Joining split XLIFF files
examples.description: Demonstrates how the input and output file markers are used to group split XLIFF files and produce joined output files. Files without the expected marker are skipped.
examples.input:
Input file marker: _PART
Output file marker: _CONCAT
Input file list:
  C:\Project\FolderABC\File1_PART001.xlf
  C:\Project\FolderABC\File1_PART002.xlf
  C:\Project\FolderABC\File2_PART001.xlf
  C:\Project\FolderABC\File2_PART002.xlf
  C:\Project\FolderABC\File3.xlf
examples.output:
C:\Project\FolderABC\File1_CONCAT.xlf
C:\Project\FolderABC\File2_CONCAT.xlf

C:\Project\FolderABC\File3.xlf -> Skipped file, the filename does not have the expected marker "_PART".

ruleTypes.description: Rule types control how elements and attributes are handled during extraction. Each element or attribute rule must have a ruleTypes array.

elementRuleTypes.INLINE: Inline element — content flows within surrounding text (e.g. <b>, <span>, <a>)
elementRuleTypes.TEXTUNIT:
Text unit — extracted as a translatable segment with skeleton before/after (e.g.

,

,
  • )
elementRuleTypes.EXCLUDE: Excluded — element and all children are skipped during extraction (e.g. <script>, <style>)
elementRuleTypes.INCLUDE: Included — exception to EXCLUDE, re-enables extraction inside an excluded block
elementRuleTypes.GROUP: Group element — structural container, not translatable itself (e.g. <table>, <ul>, <div>)
elementRuleTypes.ATTRIBUTES_ONLY: Only attributes are translatable/localizable, not the element's text content (e.g. <meta>)
elementRuleTypes.SERVER: Server element — embedded server-side content (e.g. JSP, PHP, Mason tags)

attributeRuleTypes.ATTRIBUTE_TRANS: Translatable — attribute content is extracted for translation (e.g. alt, title)
attributeRuleTypes.ATTRIBUTE_WRITABLE: Writable localizable — attribute is locale-specific and editable (e.g. href, src)
attributeRuleTypes.ATTRIBUTE_READONLY: Read-only localizable — attribute is locale-specific but not user-editable
attributeRuleTypes.ATTRIBUTE_ID: ID — attribute value is used as the segment identifier
attributeRuleTypes.ATTRIBUTE_PRESERVE_WHITESPACE: Preserve whitespace — attribute controls whitespace preservation state (e.g. xml:space)

conditions.description: Conditions are triples [attributeName, operator, value] that make extraction rules conditional on an attribute's value.

operators.EQUALS: Case-insensitive string equality. With array values, matches if attribute equals any value (OR logic).
operators.NOT_EQUALS: Case-insensitive string inequality. With array values, must not equal any of the values (AND logic).
operators.MATCHES: Java regex match. Must match the entire attribute value.

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns for detecting inline codes (placeholders, tags, format specifiers) within translatable text. Each pattern is a Java regex that matches content to be protected as an inline code.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation. Uses a custom grammar: 'if FIELD OPERATOR VALUE [and|or ...]; ...'
